topics.title: Step Turning and Grooving – Part I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Step Turning and Grooving – Part I # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Step Turning? **Step Turning** is a fundamental lathe operation in which the cutting tool advances radially (perpendicular to the workpiece axis) in discrete, equal increments to remove material from a cylindrical workpiece, producing a series of concentric **diametrical steps** of different diameters along the length of the job. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *Step turning is a turning operation carried out on a lathe to produce a stepped cylindrical workpiece, where the diameter of the workpiece is reduced in stages by moving the tool at right angles to the axis of rotation.* The **depth of cut (d)** at each step is typically **equal and uniform**, while the **length of each step (l)** is governed by the design requirement of the component. This operation is widely used to manufacture shafts, axles, pulleys, and components with multiple diameter shoulders. ## 1.2 What is Grooving? **Grooving (or Recessing)** is a related lathe operation used to cut a **narrow groove** of a specific width on the cylindrical surface of the workpiece. The groove is produced by plunging the grooving tool radially into the rotating workpiece to a precise depth. > [!NOTE] > A groove performs a **functional purpose** — it serves as a seat for circlips, oil retention, relief under a thread run-out, or as a starting/ending point for thread cutting. A grooving tool is essentially a narrow, straight cutting tool ground to the required width. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pencil Sharpener" Analogy Imagine peeling a carrot with a knife in **discrete horizontal cuts** rather than in one continuous spiral: - Each horizontal cut **reduces the diameter** of the carrot at a particular location — this is **step turning**. - The number of cuts you make equals the **number of steps** produced. - The amount you slice off at each step is the **depth of cut**. - Now, if you press the knife **fully down** at one location to make a deep narrow notch that almost severs the carrot — that is **grooving**. In engineering terms: **Step turning creates a staircase profile, while grooving creates a narrow channel on a cylindrical surface**. ## 1.4 Key Distinction Between Step Turning and Turning | Parameter | Plain Turning | Step Turning | |---|---|---| | Tool movement | Parallel to axis (longitudinal) | Perpendicular to axis (radial) | | Diameter variation | Continuous (tapered) | Discrete (stepped) | | Number of passes | Multiple, axially shifted | Multiple, radially incremental | | Output shape | Uniform diameter cylinder | Stepped cylindrical profile | > [!TIP] > **Quick Recall for Exam:** Step turning is **radial feed turning** — the tool moves **across** the diameter, not along its length. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step Turning — Operational Logic The step turning process can be broken down into the following structured sequence: - **Step 1 — Workpiece Mounting:** The raw cylindrical workpiece is held in a **3-jaw or 4-jaw chuck** with sufficient overhang. A **live center** is used on the tailstock side for long workpieces to prevent deflection. - **Step 2 — Tool Setting:** A **right-hand single-point cutting tool** (e.g., a HSS or carbide turning tool with a 0° to 15° side clearance angle) is mounted in the tool post. The **tool tip is set exactly at the center height** of the workpiece. - **Step 3 — Facing the End (Optional):** Before starting step turning, the face of the workpiece is often faced to provide a clean reference surface and a true perpendicular datum. - **Step 4 — Radial Engagement:** The tool is brought to the starting diameter, and **radial feed** is engaged. The tool is moved perpendicularly to the workpiece axis by the cross-slide handwheel. - **Step 5 — Incremental Cutting:** The tool traverses radially for a calculated **depth of cut (d)**, then returns to the original position (or the operation is continued without retraction, depending on whether multiple steps are machined in a single setup). - **Step 6 — Repetition:** Steps 4–5 are repeated until the desired final diameter at each step is achieved. - **Step 7 — Finishing:** A final pass with a **small depth of cut (0.1–0.2 mm)** is given for surface finish and dimensional accuracy. ### 2.2 Why Step Turning and Not Plain Turning? > The "why" behind choosing step turning: > - **Material Economy:** Step turning allows the original cylindrical blank to retain material at non-machined sections, preserving structural strength. > - **Multiple Diameters in One Setup:** Several diameters can be produced without changing the workpiece or tool. > - **Shoulder Production:** Square shoulders (90° to the axis) can be produced — essential for shafts with bearing seats, gear hubs, etc. > - **Reduced Setup Time:** Multiple diameters achieved in a single chucking. ## 2.3 Grooving — Operational Logic - **Step 1 — Tool Selection:** A **grooving tool** of width equal to the required groove width is mounted in the tool post. The tool has a **0° rake angle** and is ground to a precise width. - **Step 2 — Plunging:** The tool is plunged radially into the rotating workpiece using the **cross-slide** (NOT the compound slide, to avoid taper). - **Step 3 — Coolant Application:** Continuous flood coolant is applied to prevent overheating of the narrow cutting edge. - **Step 4 — Withdrawal:** After reaching the required depth, the tool is retracted radially. > [!CAUTION] > **Common Student Mistake:** Using the **compound slide** for grooving leads to a **tapered groove** because the compound slide is set at an angle. Always use the **cross-slide** for pure radial grooving. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Description | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Cutting Speed | $V = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{1000}$ | m/min | $D$ = diameter (mm), $N$ = rpm | | 2 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | Derived from cutting speed | | 3 | Feed Rate | $f_r = f \cdot N$ | mm/min | $f$ = feed per rev (mm/rev) | | 4 | Depth of Cut | $d = \dfrac{D_i - D_f}{2}$ | mm | $D_i$ = initial, $D_f$ = final diameter | | 5 | Number of Passes | $n = \dfrac{d}{d_p}$ | — | $d_p$ = depth per pass | | 6 | Machining Time (Step) | $T_m = \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ | min | $L$ = length of cut (mm) | | 7 | Material Removal Rate | $MRR = \pi \cdot D \cdot d \cdot f \cdot N$ | mm³/min | Volumetric removal | | 8 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \cdot 10^6}$ | kW | $K_c$ = specific cutting energy (N/mm²) | | 9 | Total Step Length | $L_t = l_1 + l_2 + l_3 + \dots + l_n$ | mm | Sum of all step lengths | | 10 | Total Machining Time | $T_{total} = \sum T_{m,i} + T_{setup}$ | min | Sum of individual step times + setup | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Note on K_c (Specific Cutting Energy):** For mild steel, $K_c \approx 2000 \text{ N/mm}^2$; for cast iron, $K_c \approx 1100 \text{ N/mm}^2$. These are exam-standard values to remember. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive Industry:** Crankshaft journals, camshaft bearing seats, axle shafts. - **Pump Manufacturing:** Impeller shafts, multi-stage pump rotors. - **Tooling Industry:** Arbor shafts, mandrels. - **Machine Building:** Spindle shafts, transmission shafts, coupling hubs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example — KTU Exam Standard > **Problem Statement (Model KTU Question Type):** > A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter **D₀ = 60 mm** and length **L = 200 mm** is to be step-turned to produce three diameters: > - **Step 1:** D₁ = 50 mm, length l₁ = 40 mm > - **Step 2:** D₂ = 40 mm, length l₂ = 50 mm > - **Step 3:** D₃ = 30 mm, length l₃ = 60 mm > > Given: Cutting speed V = 25 m/min, feed f = 0.2 mm/rev, depth of cut per pass d_p = 1 mm. > Find: **(a)** Spindle speed at each step, **(b)** Number of passes at each step, **(c)** Total machining time. --- ### 3.2 Part (a) — Spindle Speed at Each Step **Step 1 (at D₁ = 50 mm):** $$ N_1 = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_1} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 50} $$ $$ N_1 = \frac{25000}{157.0796} = 159.15 \text{ rpm} $$ **Rounded to standard spindle speed:** $N_1 \approx 160 \text{ rpm}$ **Step 2 (at D₂ = 40 mm):** $$ N_2 = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_2} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 40} $$ $$ N_2 = \frac{25000}{125.6637} = 198.94 \text{ rpm} $$ **Rounded:** $N_2 \approx 200 \text{ rpm}$ **Step 3 (at D₃ = 30 mm):** $$ N_3 = \frac{1000 \cdot V}{\pi \cdot D_3} = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 30} $$ $$ N_3 = \frac{25000}{94.2478} = 265.26 \text{ rpm} $$ **Rounded:** $N_3 \approx 265 \text{ rpm}$ > **Logic Note:** As the diameter decreases, the spindle speed must **increase** to maintain constant cutting speed. This is a fundamental KTU conceptual question. --- ### 3.3 Part (b) — Number of Passes at Each Step The radial depth of cut at each step is: $$ d = \frac{D_{i,\text{step}} - D_{f,\text{step}}}{2} $$ **Step 1 (60 mm → 50 mm):** $$ d_1 = \frac{60 - 50}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ mm (radial)} $$ $$ n_1 = \frac{d_1}{d_p} = \frac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ passes} $$ **Step 2 (50 mm → 40 mm):** $$ d_2 = \frac{50 - 40}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ mm (radial)} $$ $$ n_2 = \frac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ passes} $$ **Step 3 (40 mm → 30 mm):** $$ d_3 = \frac{40 - 30}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5 \text{ mm (radial)} $$ $$ n_3 = \frac{5}{1} = 5 \text{ passes} $$ --- ### 3.4 Part (c) — Total Machining Time The feed rate at each step: $$ f_{r,i} = f \cdot N_i $$ **Step 1:** $$ f_{r,1} = 0.2 \times 160 = 32 \text{ mm/min} $$ $$ T_{m,1} = \frac{l_1}{f_{r,1}} = \frac{40}{32} = 1.25 \text{ min} $$ **Step 2:** $$ f_{r,2} = 0.2 \times 200 = 40 \text{ mm/min} $$ $$ T_{m,2} = \frac{l_2}{f_{r,2}} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25 \text{ min} $$ **Step 3:** $$ f_{r,3} = 0.2 \times 265 = 53 \text{ mm/min} $$ $$ T_{m,3} = \frac{l_3}{f_{r,3}} = \frac{60}{53} = 1.132 \text{ min} $$ **Total Machining Time:** $$ T_{total} = T_{m,1} + T_{m,2} + T_{m,3} $$ $$ T_{total} = 1.25 + 1.25 + 1.132 = 3.632 \text{ min} $$ $$ \boxed{T_{total} \approx 3.63 \text{ minutes}} $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Diameter Change on Process Parameters | Parameter | Symbol | As D decreases (step turning) | KTU Exam Insight | |---|---|---|---| | Spindle speed | $N$ | **Increases** (to maintain V) | Common 3-mark question | | Cutting speed | $V$ | Constant (set value) | Define in 2 marks | | Feed rate (mm/min) | $f_r$ | Increases (if N increases) | Often confused with f | | Feed per rev | $f$ | Constant (operator set) | Distinguish clearly | | MRR | $MRR$ | Decreases (smaller D) | Apply $MRR = \pi D d f N$ | | Power | $P$ | Decreases | Lowers with smaller diameter | | Machining time per step | $T_m$ | Varies with length & N | Use $L / (f \cdot N)$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Step Turning Process Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Mount Workpiece in Chuck] --> B[Set Tool at Center Height] B --> C[Face the Workpiece End] C --> D[Calculate Spindle Speed N] D --> E[Engage Radial Feed via Cross-Slide] E --> F[Cut Pass 1: depth d_p] F --> G{More Passes Needed?} G -->|Yes| H[Return to Original Position] H --> E G -->|No| I[Final Finishing Pass] I --> J[Measure Diameter with Vernier] J --> K{Within Tolerance?} K -->|No| L[Adjust and Recut] L --> J K -->|Yes| M{More Steps to Machine?} M -->|Yes| N[Move Tool to Next Step Position] N --> D M -->|No| O[Retract Tool and Stop Spindle] O --> P[End: Remove Finished Component] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Stepped Workpiece Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Workpiece[Stepped Workpiece Profile] direction LR P1[D0 = 60 mm] -->|l1=40mm| P2[D1 = 50 mm] P2 -->|l2=50mm| P3[D2 = 40 mm] P3 -->|l3=60mm| P4[D3 = 30 mm] end R1((Step 1
d=5mm)) -.-> P2 R2((Step 2
d=5mm)) -.-> P3 R3((Step 3
d=5mm)) -.-> P4 ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Step Turning on a Centre Lathe ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph PowerTrain[Power Transmission System] PT1[Main Motor] --> PT2[Spindle Pulley] PT2 --> PT3[Spindle] end subgraph WorkHolding[Work Holding System] WH1[3-Jaw Chuck] --> WH2[Workpiece] WH2 --> WH3[Live Center on Tailstock] end PT3 --> WH1 subgraph ToolSystem[Tool Feed System] TS1[Tool Post] --> TS2[Single Point Cutting Tool] TS2 --> TS3[Cross-Slide for Radial Feed] end TS3 --> WH2 subgraph Control[Operator Control] CO1[Feed Handwheel] --> TS3 CO2[Spindle Lever] --> PT1 end CO1 -.-> TS3 CO2 -.-> PT1 ``` ## 4.4 Grooving Tool Geometry (Mermaid Cross-Section View) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Grooving Tool Front View] --> B[Tool Shank] A --> C[Cutting Edge Width = Groove Width w] A --> D[Relief Angle 5 to 10 degrees] A --> E[Tip Radius Zero for Flat Bottom] B --> F[Set at Center Height of Workpiece] F --> G[Plunge Radially into Rotating Job] G --> H[Narrow Groove Produced] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Visual Interpretation:** The above mermaid diagrams represent the **functional logic** of the step turning and grooving processes. In a KTU lab examination, you will be expected to **sketch the workpiece profile with dimensions and annotate the tool positions** on the answer sheet. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Step Turning. List any two applications. **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Step turning is a lathe operation in which the cutting tool is fed **radially (perpendicular to the axis of the workpiece)** in discrete increments to produce a series of **concentric diametrical steps** of varying diameters along the length of the cylindrical workpiece. **(2 marks)** **Applications (any two):** - Manufacturing of **multi-diameter shafts** such as crankshafts and camshafts. - Producing **shoulders on shafts** for bearing seats, gear hubs, and collars. - Machining **stepped pulleys and arbors**. **(1 mark)** --- ### Question 2: Differentiate between Step Turning and Grooving. **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Feature | Step Turning | Grooving | |---|---|---| | Purpose | Produce multiple diameters | Produce a narrow channel/groove | | Tool motion | Radial feed (multiple passes) | Radial plunge (single pass) | | Width of cut | Wide (full contact) | Narrow (equal to tool width) | | Output | Stepped cylindrical surface | A recessed groove | | Functional use | Shoulders, bearing seats | Circlip grooves, oil grooves, thread relief | **(3 marks — 1 for definition of each, 1 for distinguishing feature)** --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **(a)** A mild steel workpiece of diameter **80 mm** and length **180 mm** is to be step turned on a centre lathe to produce two steps: **D₁ = 60 mm over a length of 50 mm**, and **D₂ = 40 mm over a length of 70 mm**. The cutting speed is **V = 30 m/min**, feed **f = 0.25 mm/rev**, and depth of cut per pass **d_p = 1.5 mm**. Determine the **(i)** spindle speed at each step, **(ii)** number of passes at each step, and **(iii)** total machining time. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the procedure of step turning on a centre lathe. List the safety precautions to be observed. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **(i) Spindle Speed at each step:** For **Step 1 (D = 60 mm):** $$ N_1 = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{30000}{188.496} = 159.15 \approx 160 \text{ rpm} $$ For **Step 2 (D = 40 mm):** $$ N_2 = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{30000}{125.664} = 238.73 \approx 240 \text{ rpm} $$ **(ii) Number of Passes:** Radial depth of cut for Step 1: $$ d_1 = \frac{80 - 60}{2} = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad n_1 = \frac{10}{1.5} = 6.67 \approx 7 \text{ passes} $$ Radial depth of cut for Step 2: $$ d_2 = \frac{60 - 40}{2} = 10 \text{ mm}, \quad n_2 = \frac{10}{1.5} = 6.67 \approx 7 \text{ passes} $$ **(iii) Total Machining Time:** For Step 1: $$ T_{m,1} = \frac{50}{0.25 \times 160} = \frac{50}{40} = 1.25 \text{ min} $$ For Step 2: $$ T_{m,2} = \frac{70}{0.25 \times 240} = \frac{70}{60} = 1.167 \text{ min} $$ Total: $$ T_{total} = 1.25 + 1.167 = 2.417 \approx 2.42 \text{ min} $$ --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Procedure for Step Turning (7 marks):** 1. **Mounting the Workpiece (1 mark):** The cylindrical workpiece is mounted firmly in the 3-jaw chuck, with adequate overhang. A live center is engaged from the tailstock for long jobs to prevent deflection. 2. **Tool Setting (1 mark):** A right-hand turning tool is mounted in the tool post. The tool tip is aligned with the **center height** of the workpiece using a center gauge. The tool overhang is kept minimal. 3. **Facing the End (1 mark):** The face of the workpiece is turned perpendicular to the axis to provide a clean reference and a true datum for measuring lengths. 4. **Setting the Cross-Slide for Radial Feed (1 mark):** The cross-slide handwheel is used to feed the tool radially into the workpiece. The compound slide is set to zero degrees. 5. **Performing the Step Cuts (2 marks):** The spindle is started and brought to the calculated rpm. The tool is brought into contact, and radial feed is applied for the calculated depth per pass. Multiple passes are taken until the desired diameter is achieved. The process is repeated for subsequent steps. 6. **Safety Precautions (1 mark):** - Wear safety goggles and avoid loose clothing. - Ensure the chuck key is removed before starting the spindle. - Do not measure the workpiece while it is rotating. - Apply cutting fluid for heat dissipation. - Keep the tool post and cross-slide locking screws checked. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **grooving operation** on a lathe with a neat sketch. What are the differences between an **internal grooving tool** and an **external grooving tool**? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A workpiece of diameter **50 mm** has to be grooved to a depth of **4 mm** with a **groove width of 3 mm** using a grooving tool. The cutting speed is **V = 20 m/min** and feed **f = 0.1 mm/rev**. Calculate the **(i)** spindle speed, **(ii)** feed rate in mm/min, and **(iii)** machining time for grooving (assuming the groove is cut over a single pass with no longitudinal feed). **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Grooving Operation Explanation (4 marks):** Grooving is a lathe operation in which a **narrow cutting tool** is plunged radially into a rotating cylindrical workpiece to produce a **recess or channel** of a specific width. The width of the groove equals the width of the grooving tool. The tool is fed only in the **radial direction** using the cross-slide, not along the length. The depth is controlled precisely. Grooves are used for retaining circlips, providing oil retention, and as relief at thread run-outs. **Differences between Internal and External Grooving Tools (3 marks):** | Feature | External Grooving Tool | Internal Grooving Tool | |---|---|---| | Operation | Cuts groove on outer diameter | Cuts groove on inner diameter (bore) | | Tool shape | Straight, held in standard tool post | Cranked/bent shank to fit inside bore | | Tool holder | Standard tool post | Boring bar holder | | Application | Shaft grooves, circlip seats | Internal circlip grooves in bushings | | Approach | Direct radial access | Tool must pass through the bore opening | --- #### Model Solution for (b): **(i) Spindle Speed:** $$ N = \frac{1000 \times 20}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{20000}{157.08} = 127.32 \approx 128 \text{ rpm} $$ **(ii) Feed Rate:** $$ f_r = f \cdot N = 0.1 \times 128 = 12.8 \text{ mm/min} $$ **(iii) Machining Time:** Since grooving is purely a **radial plunge operation**, there is **no longitudinal feed**. The cutting time is determined by the radial feed rate and the depth of the groove. However, if we consider the **equivalent radial feed converted from cross-slide feed per revolution**, the time for the radial plunge is: $$ T_m = \frac{\text{Depth of groove}}{f \cdot N} = \frac{4}{0.1 \times 128} = \frac{4}{12.8} = 0.3125 \text{ min} $$ $$ \boxed{T_m \approx 18.75 \text{ seconds}} $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing depth of cut direction:** Students often use the **diametrical difference** instead of the **radial difference** for depth of cut. **Always divide by 2** for radial depth. > 2. **Forgetting unit conversion in V formula:** The $V = \pi D N / 1000$ formula requires $D$ in **mm** and gives $V$ in **m/min**. Mixing units is the #1 cause of wrong answers. > 3. **Using compound slide for grooving:** This produces a **tapered groove**, losing full marks. > 4. **Not rounding N to available spindle speeds:** Lathe spindles have discrete speed values — always round to the nearest available speed on the machine. > 5. **Confusing feed per rev (f) with feed rate (f_r):** $f$ is in mm/rev, $f_r$ is in mm/min. Examiners strictly check this distinction. > 6. **Forgetting tool center height:** If the tool is mounted above or below center, the tool geometry changes — students lose 1–2 marks for not mentioning this. --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Step turning = radial feed turning** in discrete increments. **Grooving = radial plunge** to a fixed depth. - **Master formula:** $V = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ — memorize this on Day 1. - **Spindle speed increases as diameter decreases** to maintain constant cutting speed. - **Depth of cut (radial)** $= \dfrac{D_{\text{initial}} - D_{\text{final}}}{2}$ — **never forget the divide-by-2**. - **Number of passes** $= \dfrac{\text{Total radial depth}}{\text{Depth per pass}}$ — round up to next integer. - **Machining time per step** $= \dfrac{L}{f \cdot N}$ where L is the length of that step. - **Grooving tool width = groove width** — select the tool accordingly. - **Always use the cross-slide** for radial feed in step turning and grooving; **never use the compound slide** unless intentional taper is needed. - **Tool center height** must be aligned with the workpiece center — affects tool geometry and surface finish. - **MRR** $= \pi D d f N$ and **Power** $P = \dfrac{MRR \cdot K_c}{60 \times 10^6}$ in kW. - **Specific cutting energy $K_c$:** Mild steel ≈ **2000 N/mm²**, Cast iron ≈ **1100 N/mm²**. - **Safety:** Remove chuck key, no measuring on rotating job, wear goggles, apply coolant. - **Workpiece support:** Use live center for long workpieces to avoid deflection and vibration. - **Spindle speed selection:** Always choose the **nearest available speed** on the lathe spindle — actual cutting speed will deviate slightly. - **Coolant use:** Mandatory for continuous cuts, especially in deep passes and grooving, to prevent **built-up edge** formation. - **Typical KTU exam pattern:** Expect a 14-mark calculation question on time/speed/MRR, and a 7-mark descriptive question with a sketch and safety precautions. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Mnemonic for Step Turning: "DRIVES"** — **D**iameter reduces, **R**pm **I**ncreases, **A**xis same, **V**elocity constant, **E**ach step has equal radial depth, **S**quare shoulders produced.

topics.title: Step Turning and Grooving – Part II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Step Turning and Grooving – Part II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Step Turning and Grooving – Part II** deals with the *advanced operational and analytical aspects* of producing multi-diameter cylindrical workpieces (step turning) and recessed circumferential channels (grooving) on a centre lathe using a **single-point cutting tool** (for turning) and a **narrow profiling / parting-blade tool** (for grooving).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Part II consolidates:

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Step Turning (KTU Definition):** A lathe operation in which the diameter of a workpiece is reduced in a series of discrete, stepped reductions along its longitudinal axis — each "step" being a cylindrical land of a specific length, diameter, and surface finish produced by a corresponding radial depth of cut.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **Grooving (KTU Definition):** A lathe operation in which a narrow, sharp-edged cutting tool is fed *radially inward* (external grooving) or *radially outward* (internal grooving) into a rotating workpiece to machine a shallow, rectangular channel of controlled **width (W)**, **depth (d)**, and **surface finish (Ra)** — without removing a continuous chip from the full diameter.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a **lathaped pencil sharpener** that you twist against a stationary blade — except reversed. In a centre lathe:

| Analogy Element | Lathe Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The pencil being turned by hand | **Workpiece** mounted between chuck and tailstock, rotating at N rpm |
| The sharpener blade | **Cutting tool** held in the tool post |
| Twisting the pencil into the blade | **Radial feed (cross-slide)** of the tool into the workpiece |
| The conical shape created | **Step turning** (multiple radial cuts of different depths at different Z-positions) |
| Cutting a small slot to hold an O-ring on a piston shaft | **Grooving** — feeding the tool *inward* a small distance, traversing laterally, and retracting |

For **grooving**, picture a plumber cutting a narrow rectangular notch into a metal pipe to seat a snap-ring — but with micrometre-level control, calculated spindle speed, and a documented cutting-fluid regime.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities (Bolded Standard Metrics)

> **[!NOTE]**
> The following parameters are the **four primary machining variables** the examiner expects every student to know and compute in the KTU lab viva and written exam:
> - **Cutting Speed (V):** Tangential surface velocity at the cutting point, expressed in **m/min**.
> - **Spindle Speed (N):** Rotational frequency of the spindle, expressed in **rpm**.
> - **Feed (f):** Tool advance per spindle revolution, expressed in **mm/rev**.
> - **Depth of Cut (d):** Radial penetration of the tool, expressed in **mm**.

The KTU board examiner expects these quantities to be presented with the **SI base units**, not imperial. Conversion factor: **1 m = 1000 mm** and **1 min = 60 s**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Tool Geometry for Step Turning and Grooving

### 2.1.1 Step Turning Tool
A standard **right-hand single-point turning tool (SPTT)** is used. The geometry is characterised by:
- **Nose radius (r):** Typically **0.4 mm, 0.8 mm, or 1.2 mm** — chosen based on desired surface finish and step transition.
- **Side cutting edge angle (CS):** Usually **15°** (for finishing) to **45°** (for roughing).
- **End cutting edge angle (CE):** Affects the formation of the step shoulder.
- **Side relief angle (α):** Generally **6°–10°** to prevent rubbing on the cylindrical land.

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *nose radius* with *depth of cut*. The nose radius governs **surface roughness (Ra)** and **step corner sharpness**, NOT the amount of material removed radially.

### 2.1.2 Grooving Tool
A **narrow-bladed parting/grooving tool** is used. Its critical features are:

| Feature | Typical Value | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Blade width (W) | 2, 3, 4 mm | Defines groove width |
| End cutting edge angle | 0°–5° | Prevents rubbing at groove bottom |
| Side clearance | 1°–2° per side | Allows tool to clear groove walls |
| Front clearance | 6°–10° | Prevents rubbing on groove shoulder |
| Tool material | HSS or Carbide (P20–P30) | Withstands interrupted-ish cut at groove edge |

> **[!WARNING]**
> The grooving tool has **zero (or near-zero) side cutting edge angle**, which means **all the feed force is converted to radial thrust**. This causes **deflection** — the most common reason for dimensional inaccuracy in grooving.

## 2.2 Cutting Parameter Selection Logic

### 2.2.1 Step Turning Sequence (Why Multiple Passes?)

The KTU experiment mandates a **multi-pass approach** rather than a single plunge cut because:
1. **Power limitation:** A single deep cut exceeds available spindle power.
2. **Tool wear economics:** Several light cuts extend tool life more than one heavy cut.
3. **Heat dissipation:** Multiple passes allow coolant penetration between cuts.
4. **Surface finish:** Finishing pass (light depth, high feed, sharp tool) controls Ra.

The depth of cut is selected using the **75% reduction rule** (a KTU-board-favoured heuristic):

$$d_{n+1} = 0.75 \times d_{n}$$

where $d_n$ is the depth of the $n$-th roughing pass. The final pass (finishing) is typically **0.2–0.5 mm deep**.

### 2.2.2 Grooving — Single-Pass Operation

Unlike step turning, **grooving is essentially a single-pass operation**: the tool is plunged radially to the full groove depth $d_g$ in one go (sometimes in 2 light passes for very deep grooves, e.g., $d_g > 3$ mm). The width of the groove is fixed by the blade width $W$ of the tool — no lateral feed is used for width control.

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Conceptual Distinction (Frequently Tested in KTU Viva):**
> - *Step Turning* = primarily **radial** feed (cross-slide), with stepped reductions.
> - *Grooving* = **purely radial** feed over a narrow width, no axial feed for width generation.
> - *Parting Off* = grooving all the way to the centre of the workpiece.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> The following table is the **complete formula kit** expected for the KTU University Exam (ESE) and lab viva on this topic. Memorise this verbatim.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Legend | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle Speed | $N = \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D}$ | $V$ = cutting speed; $D$ = instantaneous workpiece diameter | rpm |
| 2 | Feed Rate (linear) | $f_r = f \times N$ | $f$ = feed per rev | mm/min |
| 3 | Material Removal Rate (Turning) | $\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V$ | $d$ = depth of cut | mm³/min |
| 4 | Material Removal Rate (Grooving) | $\text{MRR}_g = W \times f \times V$ | $W$ = blade width = groove width | mm³/min |
| 5 | Machining Time (Turning) | $t_m = \dfrac{L}{f \times N}$ | $L$ = total feed length (approach + cut + overtravel) | min |
| 6 | Machining Time (Grooving) | $t_g = \dfrac{d_g}{f \times N}$ | $d_g$ = radial groove depth | min |
| 7 | Approach Length | $L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)}$ | Approximation of tool-entry arc | mm |
| 8 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{\text{MRR} \times U_p}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | $U_p$ = specific cutting energy; $\eta$ = efficiency (typically 0.8) | kW |
| 9 | Surface Roughness (theoretical) | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8 r}$ | $r$ = tool nose radius | µm |
| 10 | Number of Passes (75% rule) | $d_{n+1} = 0.75 \, d_n$ | Geometric progression | mm |

> **CRITICAL TYPESETTING NOTE:** In the table above, all absolute-value-style notation has been written using **fraction bars and parentheses only** — no vertical pipes `|` — to preserve Markdown table integrity.

| Domain | Application of Step Turning | Application of Grooving |
|---|---|---|
| Automotive | Drive shafts, axle journals | Snap-ring grooves on pistons, CV-joints |
| Hydraulic Systems | Spool valves, piston rods | O-ring grooves, seal retention |
| Tooling | Arbours, mandrels | Splitting grooves for clamping |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Spindle Speed from Cutting Speed

### 3.1.1 Physical Origin
The cutting speed $V$ is defined as the **tangential linear velocity** at the point where the tool meets the workpiece surface. If a point on a rotating cylinder of diameter $D$ travels one full revolution, it covers a circumferential distance equal to the circumference:

$$\text{Circumference} = \pi \, D \quad (\text{mm per revolution})$$

If the spindle makes $N$ revolutions per minute, the linear distance travelled per minute is:

$$\text{Distance per minute} = \pi \, D \times N \quad (\text{mm/min})$$

By definition, this distance per minute IS the cutting speed $V$ (in mm/min). To express $V$ in m/min (the standard KTU unit), we convert using $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$:

$$V \text{ (m/min)} = \dfrac{\pi \, D \text{ (mm)} \times N \text{ (rpm)}}{1000}$$

Rearranging algebraically to solve for $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\pi \, D \, N &= 1000 \, V \\
N &= \dfrac{1000 \, V}{\pi \, D} \quad \text{(revolutions per minute, rpm)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental KTU spindle speed equation**.

### 3.1.2 Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** A mild steel workpiece of initial diameter $D_0 = 50$ mm is to be turned at a cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min. Find the spindle speed for the **first roughing pass** when the diameter is still effectively $D_0$.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50} \\
&= \dfrac{30000}{157.08} \\
&= 190.99 \text{ rpm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Rounded to the nearest spindle-step (typical lathes: 40, 50, 63, 80, 100, 125, 160, 200, 250 …): **$N = 200$ rpm**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Machining Time for Step Turning

### 3.2.1 Length Components
The total tool travel $L$ along the workpiece axis consists of three additive components:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= L_a + L_w + L_o \\
\end{aligned}
$$

where:
- $L_a$ = **approach length** (the length the tool travels *before* the start diameter is fully engaged, due to the tool-nose radius arc).
- $L_w$ = **workpiece length** being machined.
- $L_o$ = **overtravel** (the length the tool travels *past* the end of the workpiece to allow for the tool exit and clearing, typically 2–3 mm).

### 3.2.2 Approach Length — Geometric Derivation

When a tool with nose radius $r$ is fed into a cylindrical workpiece of original diameter $D$ to a depth of cut $d$, the tool "rounds in" over a length equal to the horizontal projection of the nose-arc engagement. The exact geometry (ignoring the small nose radius $r \ll D$) gives the approximation:

$$
L_a = \sqrt{d \, (D - d)}
$$

**Derivation:** Consider the tool-tip centre at height $(D/2 - d)$ above the workpiece centre. The nose radius arc enters the workpiece where the cylindrical surface meets the tool flank. Solving the circle-line intersection in the $(x, y)$ plane with the tool centre offset by $d$ from the original surface, the horizontal chord length of the engagement arc is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } h &= d \quad \text{(radial depth of cut)} \\
\text{Original radius} &= R = \frac{D}{2} \\
\text{New radius after cut} &= R - d \\
\text{Chord of intersection} &= 2 \sqrt{R^2 - (R - d)^2} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{R^2 - R^2 + 2Rd - d^2} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{2Rd - d^2} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{d \, (2R - d)} \\
&= 2 \sqrt{d \, (D - d)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a single-engagement tool, only **half** of this chord is traversed as approach (the rest is part of the cut). Hence the standard KTU formula uses:

$$
L_a = \sqrt{d \, (D - d)}
$$

### 3.2.3 Machining Time Equation
Since the tool advances $f$ mm per spindle revolution, the time to traverse length $L$ at $N$ rpm is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m &= \dfrac{L}{f \times N} \\
&= \dfrac{L_a + L_w + L_o}{f \times N} \quad \text{(minutes)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.4 Worked Numerical Example
**Given:** Turning a mild-steel shaft of initial diameter $D = 50$ mm, final diameter $D_f = 44$ mm, length $L_w = 60$ mm, feed $f = 0.2$ mm/rev, cutting speed $V = 30$ m/min, overtravel $L_o = 3$ mm.

**Step 1 — Compute depth of cut:**
$$d = \frac{D - D_f}{2} = \frac{50 - 44}{2} = 3 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed (using initial diameter since this is the first pass):**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 30}{\pi \times 50} = 190.99 \approx 191 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 3 — Approach length:**
$$L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)} = \sqrt{3 \times (50 - 3)} = \sqrt{3 \times 47} = \sqrt{141} = 11.87 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total length:**
$$L = L_a + L_w + L_o = 11.87 + 60 + 3 = 74.87 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_m &= \dfrac{74.87}{0.2 \times 191} \\
&= \dfrac{74.87}{38.2} \\
&= 1.96 \text{ minutes} \approx 1 \text{ min } 58 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Machining Time for Grooving (Deduced from First Principles)

The grooving tool does not traverse axially across the full workpiece length. It plunges **radially** by an amount $d_g$ (the groove depth). The total "feed length" along the *radial direction* is therefore $d_g$ (plus a small clearance overshoot, typically $0.2$–$0.5$ mm, to ensure the groove bottom is clean).

By the same first-principles argument as for turning:

$$
t_g = \dfrac{d_g}{f \times N} \quad \text{(minutes, single pass)}
$$

**If the groove is done in two passes** (e.g., roughing plunge to $0.7 \, d_g$, then finishing to full $d_g$):

$$
t_{g,\text{total}} = \dfrac{0.7 \, d_g}{f \times N_1} + \dfrac{d_g}{f \times N_2}
$$

where $N_1, N_2$ are the spindle speeds at the workpiece outer diameter (essentially the same value in both passes since the workpiece diameter is virtually unchanged during a small radial plunge).

### Worked Example — Grooving
**Given:** Workpiece diameter at groove location $D = 46$ mm, groove depth $d_g = 2.5$ mm, blade width $W = 3$ mm, feed $f = 0.08$ mm/rev, cutting speed $V = 25$ m/min.

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 46} = \frac{25000}{144.51} = 173.0 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Machining time (single pass, ignoring overshoot):**
$$t_g = \frac{2.5}{0.08 \times 173} = \frac{2.5}{13.84} = 0.181 \text{ min} \approx 10.8 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Material removal rate (grooving-specific):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MRR}_g &= W \times f \times V \\
&= 3 \times 0.08 \times 25 \times 1000 \quad \text{(note: convert V to mm/min)} \\
&= 3 \times 0.08 \times 25000 \\
&= 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Step Turning vs. Grooving

> **[!NOTE]**
> This matrix is a **high-yield summary** for the KTU viva. Examiners often ask students to *contrast* the two operations in a single answer.

| Attribute | Step Turning | Grooving |
|---|---|---|
| Tool type | SPTT with nose radius | Narrow parting-style blade |
| Primary feed direction | Radial (cross-slide) | Radial (cross-slide) |
| Number of passes | Multiple (75% rule) | One (or two for deep grooves) |
| Feed per rev (typical) | 0.1 – 0.4 mm/rev | 0.03 – 0.12 mm/rev (lower!) |
| Cutting speed (mild steel) | 25 – 35 m/min (HSS) | 20 – 30 m/min (HSS) |
| Dominant force | Tangential + radial | Radial (very high thrust) |
| Surface finish control | Nose radius + feed | Blade-edge sharpness + feed |
| Chip shape | Continuous (long) | C-shaped / segmented (narrow) |
| Cooling | Flood recommended | Flood **essential** (heat concentration) |
| MRR formula | $f \times d \times V$ | $W \times f \times V$ |
| Time formula | $L / (f \times N)$ | $d_g / (f \times N)$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Differentiate between step turning and grooving operations on a centre lathe. Mention any two distinguishing parameters.**

**Model Answer:**

Step turning is a lathe operation in which the diameter of a rotating cylindrical workpiece is reduced progressively along its length in a series of discrete, coaxial cylindrical lands of differing diameters. The tool is a standard single-point turning tool (SPTT) with a nose radius, and cutting occurs over the full cylindrical surface at each step.

Grooving, in contrast, is the operation of cutting a **narrow, shallow rectangular recess** on the surface of a rotating workpiece using a **narrow-bladed grooving tool**. The tool plunges radially into the workpiece and does not traverse axially to remove a long continuous chip.

**Two distinguishing parameters:**

1. **Feed direction:** Step turning uses a *radial depth of cut* combined with *axial traverse*; grooving uses *purely radial feed* with no axial feed for groove-width generation.
2. **MRR formula:** $\text{MRR}_{\text{turning}} = f \times d \times V$, whereas $\text{MRR}_{\text{grooving}} = W \times f \times V$, where $W$ is the blade width (= groove width).

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Why is the feed rate kept lower in grooving as compared to step turning?**

**Model Answer:**

The feed rate is kept lower in grooving because:

1. **Limited contact area:** The grooving tool has a very small blade width $W$ (typically 2–4 mm), so the contact area between tool and chip is small. Higher feeds would generate excessive **specific cutting energy** and heat concentration at the tool edge.

2. **Radial thrust dominance:** A grooving tool has zero (or near-zero) side cutting edge angle, so the entire cutting force acts **radially**, causing tool deflection. Lower feed reduces this thrust and improves groove-dimension accuracy.

3. **Tool-rigidity constraint:** A narrow blade has lower section modulus; a high feed would cause the blade to **chatter, deflect, or break**.

4. **Surface finish:** Grooves are often functional (seating O-rings, snap-rings) and require a controlled bottom finish; a lower feed yields a better $R_a$ value at the groove base.

Typical feed: **0.03–0.12 mm/rev** for grooving vs. **0.1–0.4 mm/rev** for turning.

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[!NOTE]**

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**

A mild steel shaft of initial diameter **60 mm** is to be reduced to a diameter of **52 mm** over a length of **80 mm** using a centre lathe. The cutting speed recommended is **28 m/min** (HSS tool) and the feed is **0.25 mm/rev**. A groove of **3 mm width** and **2 mm depth** is to be cut at the midpoint of the turned portion. Calculate:

**(a)** [7 Marks] The spindle speed, approach length, total feed length, and machining time for the **step turning** operation. Also compute the MRR.

**(b)** [7 Marks] The spindle speed at the groove location, the feed rate in mm/min, the machining time for the grooving pass, and the MRR for grooving. State any assumption made.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Step Turning

**Given:**
- $D_0 = 60$ mm, $D_f = 52$ mm, $L_w = 80$ mm, $V = 28$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/rev, $L_o = 3$ mm.

**Step 1 — Depth of cut:**
$$d = \frac{D_0 - D_f}{2} = \frac{60 - 52}{2} = 4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Spindle speed (using $D_0$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \dfrac{1000 \times 28}{\pi \times 60} = \dfrac{28000}{188.50} = 148.54 \text{ rpm} \\
&\approx 150 \text{ rpm (nearest available spindle step)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Approach length:**
$$L_a = \sqrt{d(D_0 - d)} = \sqrt{4 \times (60 - 4)} = \sqrt{4 \times 56} = \sqrt{224} = 14.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 — Total feed length:**
$$L = L_a + L_w + L_o = 14.97 + 80 + 3 = 97.97 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Machining time:**
$$t_m = \frac{L}{f \times N} = \frac{97.97}{0.25 \times 150} = \frac{97.97}{37.5} = 2.61 \text{ min} \approx 2 \text{ min } 37 \text{ s}$$

**Step 6 — MRR:**
$$\text{MRR} = f \times d \times V = 0.25 \times 4 \times 28000 = 28000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 28 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Grooving

**Given:**
- $D = 52$ mm (diameter at groove location, after step turning), $W = 3$ mm, $d_g = 2$ mm, $V = 25$ m/min (slightly lower for grooving — assumption), $f = 0.08$ mm/rev (assumed lower for grooving).

**Assumption stated:** The grooving tool is HSS with recommended $V = 25$ m/min and a typical feed of $0.08$ mm/rev. Coolant is used (flood).

**Step 1 — Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 25}{\pi \times 52} = \frac{25000}{163.36} = 153.0 \text{ rpm}$$

**Step 2 — Feed rate (linear):**
$$f_r = f \times N = 0.08 \times 153 = 12.24 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 3 — Machining time (single pass, with 0.3 mm overshoot):**
$$L_{g,\text{total}} = d_g + 0.3 = 2.3 \text{ mm}$$
$$t_g = \frac{2.3}{0.08 \times 153} = \frac{2.3}{12.24} = 0.188 \text{ min} \approx 11.3 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — MRR (grooving-specific):**
$$\text{MRR}_g = W \times f \times V = 3 \times 0.08 \times 25000 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question A):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using the *final* diameter ($D_f = 52$ mm) to compute spindle speed for the *first* roughing pass. **Correction:** Use $D_0 = 60$ mm for the first pass.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to convert $V$ from m/min to mm/min when computing MRR. **Correction:** Multiply $V$ by 1000 in the MRR formula.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Confusing the MRR formulas for turning vs. grooving. **Correction:** Grooving uses blade width $W$ in place of depth of cut $d$.

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks): **[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme, Alternative]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the help of a labelled sketch, describe the **tool geometry of a grooving tool**. Explain why a grooving tool is more prone to **deflection and breakage** than a standard turning tool, and state **two remedies** for the same.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A workpiece of **40 mm diameter** is to have a **groove of 4 mm width and 2.5 mm depth** machined on its surface. Given cutting speed $V = 22$ m/min and feed $f = 0.1$ mm/rev, calculate:
   - (i) Spindle speed at the groove location.
   - (ii) Linear feed rate in mm/min.
   - (iii) Machining time for the grooving operation (assume 0.5 mm overshoot).
   - (iv) MRR for the grooving operation.

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Tool Geometry & Deflection Analysis

**Labelled Features of a Grooving Tool (description, since sketch is textual):**

1. **Shank:** Rectangular mild-steel body, typically $12 \times 12$ mm or $16 \times 16$ mm cross-section, holding the cutting insert/blade.
2. **Blade (Nose):** The narrow forward projection that performs cutting. Its **width $W$** defines the groove width.
3. **Cutting Edge:** The two side edges of the blade (left and right) and the front edge — all have **small clearance angles (1°–2°)** to prevent rubbing.
4. **Side Relief Angles:** Provided on both sides of the blade.
5. **End Cutting Edge Angle:** Typically 0°–5°, allowing the tool to reach the bottom of the groove.
6. **Nose Radius:** Effectively zero (sharp) at the cutting edge — this is what makes grooving difficult.
7. **Tool Material:** HSS (M2 / M42) for general use; Carbide (P20, P30) for production.

**Why a Grooving Tool Deflects / Breaks More Easily:**

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| Narrow blade cross-section | Low section modulus → high bending stress for a given radial force |
| Zero side cutting edge angle | All cutting force is converted to radial thrust, no component along feed to "share" the load |
| Long overhang | Tool is held only at the shank; blade is far from support, magnifying deflection |
| Heat concentration | All cutting energy is concentrated at a small contact area |
| Vibration tendency | Lack of rake face area reduces damping; tool can chatter |

**Two Remedies:**

1. **Reduce feed rate** — lower radial force, lower stress, lower heat input.
2. **Use a rigid tool setup** — minimise tool overhang, use a wider shank, use a carbide tool with positive rake.
3. (Additional) **Use a grooving tool with a chip-breaker geometry** to break the chip and prevent re-cutting.
4. (Additional) **Apply flood coolant** to reduce thermal softening.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Grooving Numerical

**Given:** $D = 40$ mm, $W = 4$ mm, $d_g = 2.5$ mm, $V = 22$ m/min, $f = 0.1$ mm/rev, overshoot = 0.5 mm.

**(i) Spindle speed:**
$$N = \frac{1000 \times 22}{\pi \times 40} = \frac{22000}{125.66} = 175.07 \text{ rpm}$$

**(ii) Linear feed rate:**
$$f_r = f \times N = 0.1 \times 175.07 = 17.51 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Machining time:**
$$L_g = d_g + 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ mm}$$
$$t_g = \frac{3.0}{0.1 \times 175.07} = \frac{3.0}{17.51} = 0.1714 \text{ min} \approx 10.3 \text{ s}$$

**(iv) MRR (grooving):**
$$\text{MRR}_g = W \times f \times V = 4 \times 0.1 \times 22000 = 8800 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 8.8 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}$$

> **[!WARNING]**
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Listing tool features without explaining the *function* of each. The KTU 2024 rubric allocates marks for the **engineering justification** of each feature, not just enumeration.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to include the **overshoot** in the total feed length. Without it, the groove bottom may not be cleanly finished.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting spindle speed to excessive decimal places (e.g., 175.07 rpm) without rounding to the **nearest available spindle step on the actual machine**. Examiners expect a sensible rounded value (e.g., 175 rpm or 180 rpm).

---

## ✦ TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **[!IMPORTANT]**
> The following bullet-list is a **high-density rapid-revision checklist**. Cover every item before entering the KTU lab or writing the exam.

- **Spindle speed formula:** $N = 1000 V / (\pi D)$ — always use the **diameter at the cutting point** (use $D_0$ for the first pass, $D_f$ for the last pass).
- **Feed rate formula:** $f_r = f \times N$ — gives mm/min, not mm/rev.
- **MRR (turning):** $f \times d \times V$ — note that $V$ must be in **mm/min** (multiply m/min by 1000).
- **MRR (grooving):** $W \times f \times V$ — the **groove width $W$ replaces the depth of cut $d$**.
- **Machining time (turning):** $t_m = (L_a + L_w + L_o) / (f \times N)$ — never forget the **approach** and **overtravel** terms.
- **Machining time (grooving):** $t_g = d_g / (f \times N)$ — radial plunge only, plus overshoot.
- **Approach length:** $L_a = \sqrt{d(D - d)}$ — derived from circle-line geometry.
- **75% reduction rule:** Successive roughing pass depths follow $d_{n+1} = 0.75 \, d_n$; finishing pass is **0.2–0.5 mm** light.
- **Tool deflection in grooving:** Always lower the feed; use a rigid setup; consider carbide for production.
- **Feed for turning:** **0.1–0.4 mm/rev** for mild steel; **Feed for grooving:** **0.03–0.12 mm/rev**.
- **Coolant:** Mandatory (flood) for both operations; especially critical in grooving due to heat concentration.
- **Spindle speed rounding:** Always round to the nearest available spindle step (e.g., 100, 125, 160, 200 rpm on a typical geared-head lathe).
- **Unit consistency:** $V$ in m/min for $N$ calculation; $V$ in mm/min for MRR calculation. Do NOT mix.
- **Grooving tool blade width = groove width:** There is NO axial feed used to generate groove width.
- **Theoretical surface roughness:** $R_a = f^2 / (8r)$ — higher nose radius and lower feed → better finish.
- **Common lab defects to identify and report:** Taper (tool deflection), chatter (low rigidity), oversize groove (overshoot too large), poor surface finish (high feed, worn tool), burr at groove edge (incorrect end cutting edge angle).
- **Safety recap:** Always retract tool before stopping spindle; never measure a rotating workpiece; wear safety goggles; ensure chuck key is removed before starting.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Taper Turning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2 — Taper Turning on a Lathe

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Taper Turning** is defined as the operation of producing a **uniform conical surface** on a cylindrical workpiece by gradually reducing its diameter from one end to the other while it is being rotated on the lathe spindle. In technical terms, the operation transforms a parallel-sided cylindrical job into a frustum of a cone (a truncated cone).

The standard KTU textbook (Ghosh & Mallik, Manufacturing Science / Pandey & Shan, textbook on Machining) defines the relevant geometric quantities as follows:

- **Taper (T):** The rate of change of diameter per unit length of the workpiece.
- **Taper Ratio (C):** Half the taper value; mathematically, $C = T/2$.
- **Included Angle (α):** The angle subtended at the apex of the imaginary complete cone.
- **Half Included Angle (θ):** The angle the tapered surface makes with the axis of the workpiece; $\theta = \alpha/2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Machine Tool Lab (PCPEL407), students are expected to (a) produce a specified taper on a mild steel workpiece using **at least two methods**, (b) measure the taper using a **sine bar / vernier bevel protractor / dial indicator**, and (c) verify against the **specified dimensional tolerance** (±0.05 mm typical).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Road Divider" Concept

Imagine the **median divider** of a highway that gradually narrows as it approaches a toll booth. The width reduces uniformly over a fixed length. Now picture a steel shaft rotating in a lathe chuck — if the cutting tool is fed such that its path **angles inward** along the job length, the resulting shape is exactly that of a tapered cone, just like the road divider.

Another intuitive analogy: the **pointed end of a sharpened pencil**. If you rotate the pencil between centers and feed a blade at an angle, you produce a tapered profile. The steeper the angle, the more pronounced the taper.

## 1.3 Why Taper Turning? — Engineering Necessity

> [!NOTE]
> Tapered surfaces serve **three critical engineering functions:**
> 1. **Self-holding alignment** — Morse tapers in drill shanks, lathe spindle noses, and tool holders lock firmly under axial load without keys or threads.
> 2. **Easy assembly/disassembly** — Tapered joints (e.g., between arbors and grinder spindles) can be released by a single axial blow.
> 3. **Load transmission** — Tapered roller bearings and machine tool spindles transmit both radial and axial loads efficiently through wedging action.

Everyday examples: **drill shanks, machine tool arbors, lathe centers, valve stems, pipe ends, bullet casings, and gear shafts** — all rely on precise taper turning.

## 1.4 Geometric Quantities of a Tapered Job

Consider a workpiece of length $L$, large diameter $D$ (at the tailstock end), and small diameter $d$ (at the chuck end):

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit |
|--------|----------|------|
| $D$ | Larger diameter | mm |
| $d$ | Smaller diameter | mm |
| $L$ | Length of tapered portion | mm |
| $T$ | Taper (per unit length) | mm/mm |
| $\alpha$ | Included cone angle | degrees |
| $\theta$ | Half included angle | degrees |

The taper is calculated as:

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L}
$$

The half-angle $\theta$ that the tapered surface makes with the axis:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{T}{2}
$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methods of Taper Turning — Comparative Overview

KTU expects students to be conversant with **five primary methods**. Each method produces the same geometric result but differs in accuracy, setup time, length limitation, and tooling cost.

| S.No. | Method | Best Suited For | Max Length | Typical Accuracy |
|-------|--------|----------------|------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Form Tool Method** | Short, steep tapers (< 25 mm) | < 25 mm | ±0.05 mm |
| 2 | **Compound Slide (Swivel Slide) Method** | Short tapers, internal & external | Up to 200 mm | ±0.03 mm |
| 3 | **Tailstock Set-Over Method** | Long, gentle tapers | Full bed length | ±0.10 mm |
| 4 | **Taper Turning Attachment Method** | Long, accurate tapers | Up to 300 mm | ±0.02 mm |
| 5 | **CNC Lathe (Direct Programming)** | Any taper, complex profiles | Program-controlled | ±0.01 mm |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Tip:** In lab examinations, **Compound Slide Method** and **Tailstock Set-Over Method** are the two most commonly tested. Be prepared with calculations for both.

## 2.2 Detailed Working of Each Method

### Method 1 — Form Tool Method
- The cutting tool is ground to the exact taper angle.
- Fed radially into the workpiece; no longitudinal feed is required.
- **Limitation:** Only suitable for very short tapers (because ground form tools become long and weak beyond 25 mm).

### Method 2 — Compound Slide (Swivel) Method
- The compound rest (top slide) is graduated and can be swiveled.
- The top slide is rotated by an angle $\theta$ from the cross-slide axis.
- Feeding the compound slide along its axis produces the taper.
- **Setting formula:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip:** The compound slide travel is limited (~100–150 mm on most lathes). Hence this method works for **short tapers only**, but it is the **most accurate manual method**.

### Method 3 — Tailstock Set-Over Method
- The tailstock body is displaced laterally (perpendicular to the lathe axis) by an amount called **setover ($x$)**.
- This makes the workpiece axis inclined to the tool feed path.
- **Setting formula:**

$$
x = \frac{L \times T}{2} = \frac{L}{2} \times \frac{D-d}{L} = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often wrongly substitute the full workpiece length $L$ including non-tapered portions. The $L$ in the formula refers **only to the tapered length**, not the total job length.

### Method 4 — Taper Turning Attachment Method
- A **universal taper attachment** is mounted on the lathe carriage.
- It consists of a **sine bar** (or guide bar) and a **slide block** connected by a tie rod.
- The guide bar is set at an angle $\theta$ such that $\tan(\theta) = (D-d)/(2L)$.
- As the carriage moves longitudinally, the cross-slide is automatically displaced to follow the angled guide.
- **Advantage:** The carriage power feed can be used.

### Method 5 — CNC Method
- Coordinates of start and end diameters are programmed.
- Taper produced automatically by linear interpolation (G01 with X, Z coordinates).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Ready)

| # | Formula | Used In | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $T = (D - d)/L$ | All methods | mm/mm |
| 2 | $\tan(\theta) = (D - d)/(2L)$ | Compound slide, Taper attachment | radians → degrees |
| 3 | $x = L \cdot T / 2$ | Tailstock setover | mm |
| 4 | $\theta = \tan^{-1}((D - d)/(2L))$ | Angle setting | degrees |
| 5 | $\alpha = 2\theta$ | Included angle | degrees |
| 6 | $D = d + (T \times L)$ | Reverse calculation (find D) | mm |
| 7 | $\text{TPI (Taper per inch)} = T \times 25.4$ | For imperial tapers | mm/inch |
| 8 | Morse Taper TPI values | Standard tapers | mm/inch |

> [!NOTE]
> **Morse Taper Standard Reference (commonly asked in viva):**
> - MT0 = 1:19.21 (≈ 1.49°)
> - MT1 = 1:20.047 (≈ 1.43°)
> - MT2 = 1:20.020 (≈ 1.43°)
> - MT3 = 1:19.222 (≈ 1.49°)

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Taper turning is foundational to **interchangeable manufacturing**. Every standard taper (Morse, Brown & Sharpe, Jarno, Jacobs) ensures that tools, arbors, and spindles from any manufacturer can be interchanged. Without standardized taper turning, modular tooling systems like **CAT, BT, and HSK** for CNC machines would not exist. In aerospace, tapered shafts are used in **turbine assemblies** for vibration damping; in automotive, **axles and steering columns** rely on tapered fits.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Taper Angle from Diameter and Length

**Problem Setup:** Given a workpiece of large diameter $D$, small diameter $d$, and tapered length $L$, derive an expression for the half-angle $\theta$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Progression

**Step 1 — Visualize the geometry.**
Consider the tapered portion as a frustum of a cone. The longitudinal axis of the workpiece is the axis of the cone. The tapered surface, when extended, would meet the axis at the imaginary apex.

**Step 2 — Identify the right triangle.**
Drop a perpendicular from a point on the larger-diameter edge to the smaller-diameter edge. This forms a right triangle whose:
- Opposite side = $(D - d)/2$ (radius difference)
- Adjacent side = $L$ (length of tapered portion)
- Angle at the axis = $\theta$ (half included angle)

**Step 3 — Apply trigonometry.**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{\text{Opposite}}{\text{Adjacent}} = \frac{(D-d)/2}{L}
$$

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$.**

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Find the full included angle.**

$$
\alpha = 2\theta = 2 \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{D - d}{2L}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **foundation formula** for both Compound Slide and Taper Attachment methods. Memorize it without error.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tailstock Set-Over Formula

**Problem Setup:** When the tailstock is offset laterally by a distance $x$, derive the relation between setover, taper, and length.

### Step-by-Step Logical Progression

**Step 1 — Understand the geometry.**
After setover, the workpiece axis is no longer parallel to the tool feed direction (longitudinal carriage travel). It makes an angle $\theta$ such that:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{x}{L}
$$

**Step 2 — Relate taper to the angle.**
From the previous derivation, $\tan(\theta) = (D-d)/(2L)$.

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $\tan(\theta)$.**

$$
\frac{x}{L} = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $x$.**

$$
x = \frac{L \cdot (D - d)}{2L} = \frac{D - d}{2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This simplification shows that **setover depends only on the diameter difference**, not on the length — provided the workpiece length $L$ equals the length over which the taper extends (and equals the distance between centers).

**Step 5 — Express setover in terms of taper $T$.**

$$
x = \frac{L \cdot T}{2}
$$

where $T = (D-d)/L$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Pattern)

### Example 1 — Compound Slide Method

> **Question:** A taper of length **120 mm** is to be turned on a workpiece. The large diameter is **50 mm** and the small diameter is **40 mm**. Calculate the angle to which the compound slide must be set.

**Solution:**

Given: $D = 50$ mm, $d = 40$ mm, $L = 120$ mm.

Step 1 — Calculate taper:

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{50 - 40}{120} = \frac{10}{120} = 0.0833 \text{ mm/mm}
$$

Step 2 — Calculate $\tan(\theta)$:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \times 120} = \frac{10}{240} = 0.04167
$$

Step 3 — Find $\theta$:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.04167) = 2.387^\circ
$$

**Answer:** Set compound slide to **2° 23′** (i.e., 2 degrees and 23 minutes).

---

### Example 2 — Tailstock Set-Over Method

> **Question:** A shaft of total length **200 mm** is to have a taper of length **180 mm**. The large diameter is **60 mm** and the small diameter is **48 mm**. Calculate the tailstock setover required.

**Solution:**

Given: $L = 180$ mm, $D = 60$ mm, $d = 48$ mm.

Step 1 — Calculate taper:

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{60 - 48}{180} = \frac{12}{180} = 0.0667 \text{ mm/mm}
$$

Step 2 — Apply the setover formula:

$$
x = \frac{L \times T}{2} = \frac{180 \times 0.0667}{2} = \frac{12.0}{2} = 6.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Answer:** Setover = **6.0 mm**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Examiners often catch students using total length 200 mm instead of tapered length 180 mm. Always re-read the problem to confirm which length to use.

---

### Example 3 — Form Tool Method (Verification)

> **Question:** Verify the form tool width required for a taper of length **20 mm**, large diameter **45 mm**, small diameter **35 mm**.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Half-angle:

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{45 - 35}{2 \times 20}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{10}{40}\right) = \tan^{-1}(0.25) = 14.04^\circ
$$

Step 2 — Width of form tool = Length of tapered portion = **20 mm**.

Step 3 — Form tool is ground at angle $\alpha = 2 \times 14.04 = 28.07^\circ$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Method to Choose?

| Selection Criterion | Best Method | Reason |
|---------------------|-------------|--------|
| Taper length < 25 mm, steep | Form Tool | Simplicity, single-pass |
| Taper length < 150 mm, high accuracy | Compound Slide | Direct angle setting |
| Taper length > 150 mm, gentle slope | Tailstock Set-Over | Long travel possible |
| High volume production | Taper Attachment | Uses power feed |
| Complex multi-taper profile | CNC | Programmable |
| Internal taper required | Compound Slide | Only manual method feasible |
| Very small lot size, no tooling | Tailstock Set-Over | No special attachments |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied
All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `n1`, `n2`, `nA`); no reserved Mermaid keywords are used as standalone IDs. Labels contain raw alphanumeric text without markdown formatting.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Taper Turning Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Mount Workpiece in Lathe Chuck] --> B[Set Spindle Speed RPM Based on Material]
    B --> C{Select Taper Turning Method}
    C --> D1[Method 1 Form Tool: Grind Tool to Taper Angle]
    C --> D2[Method 2 Compound Slide: Swivel Top Slide by Theta]
    C --> D3[Method 3 Tailstock Setover: Offset Tailstock by x]
    C --> D4[Method 4 Taper Attachment: Set Sine Bar Angle]
    C --> D5[Method 5 CNC: Program G01 with XZ Coordinates]
    D1 --> E[Engage Feed: Radial Feed for Form Tool]
    D2 --> F[Engage Feed: Longitudinal Feed Along Compound Slide Axis]
    D3 --> G[Engage Feed: Longitudinal Power Feed]
    D4 --> H[Engage Feed: Automatic Cross Slide Displacement]
    D5 --> I[CNC Executes Linear Interpolation]
    E --> J[Measure Taper Using Sine Bar or Bevel Protractor]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Taper Within Tolerance?}
    K -->|No| L[Adjust Tool Position and Recut]
    L --> J
    K -->|Yes| M[End: Taper Turned Job Ready for Inspection]
```

## 4.3 Geometric Schematic — Tapered Workpiece (ASCII Representation)

```
        D (large dia.)                          d (small dia.)
         |                                       |
         |<- - - - - - - - L (taper length) - - - - - ->|
         +---------------------------------------+
        / \                                     /
       /   \                                   /
      /     \                                 /
     /       \  alpha (included angle)      /
    / theta   \                             /
   /___________X___________________________X
   (apex of imaginary cone)
```

**Reading the schematic:**
- $D$ = larger diameter end (closer to tailstock in set-over method)
- $d$ = smaller diameter end (closer to chuck)
- $L$ = axial length over which taper occurs
- $\alpha$ = included angle (full cone apex angle)
- $\theta = \alpha/2$ = angle compound slide makes with axis

## 4.4 Schematic — Tailstock Set-Over Geometry

```
   Lathe Bed  ================================ (horizontal)
                    |                ^
                    |                |  x (setover)
   Tailstock axis  |               /
                    |              / theta
                    v             / 
                   [WS]==========X===========[CHUCK]
                    ^                       ^
                    |                       |
                    L (taper length)
```

When the tailstock is offset laterally by $x$, the workpiece axis tilts at angle $\theta$. The cutting tool moves horizontally; the workpiece surface "presents" a tapered profile to the tool.

## 4.5 Setup Diagram — Compound Slide Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Lathe_Assembly
        A[Chuck Holding Workpiece] --> B[Carriage with Cross Slide]
        B --> C[Compound Slide on Top of Cross Slide]
        C --> D[Compound Slide Swiveled by Theta from Cross Axis]
        D --> E[Tool Post Holding Cutting Tool]
    end
    F[Feed Direction Along Compound Slide Axis] --> E
    G[Component of Feed Produces Taper] --> H[Tapered Workpiece Surface]
```

## 4.6 Process Flow Matrix for Lab Viva

| Step | Action | Tool/Instrument Used |
|------|--------|----------------------|
| 1 | Identify large & small diameter ends | Vernier Caliper |
| 2 | Measure $L$ | Scale / Vernier |
| 3 | Compute $T$ and $\theta$ | Calculator / Standard Tables |
| 4 | Set tool height to center | Centre Gauge |
| 5 | Set compound slide angle | Built-in protractor scale |
| 6 | Make rough cut, then finish cut | HSS Cutting Tool |
| 7 | Measure taper produced | Sine Bar + Slip Gauges + Dial Indicator |
| 8 | Verify against specification | Comparison with drawing |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define taper. List any three methods of producing taper on a lathe.**

**Model Answer:**

Taper is defined as the **uniform gradual decrease in diameter along the length of a cylindrical workpiece**. Mathematically, $T = (D - d)/L$, where $D$ is the larger diameter, $d$ is the smaller diameter, and $L$ is the length over which the taper occurs.

Three methods of producing taper on a lathe are:
1. **Compound Slide Method** — top slide is swiveled by half-angle $\theta$.
2. **Tailstock Set-Over Method** — tailstock is offset laterally by $x = L \cdot T / 2$.
3. **Taper Turning Attachment Method** — uses a sine bar-driven cross slide.

*(Other acceptable methods: Form Tool Method, CNC Method.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: What is meant by 'taper ratio'? How is it different from taper?**

**Model Answer:**

The **taper ratio (C)** is defined as half the taper value. It is the ratio of the difference in diameters to twice the length:

$$
C = \frac{T}{2} = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

The **taper** refers to the **change in diameter per unit length** of the workpiece, while the **taper ratio** refers to the **change in radius per unit length** (or equivalently, the tangent of the half-angle). For standard tapers (e.g., Morse tapers), the ratio is conventionally used; for example, MT2 has a taper ratio of approximately **1:20**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common student mistakes costing marks:
> - **Unit confusion:** Mixing degrees and radians when finding $\theta$.
> - **Length mis-substitution:** Using total length instead of tapered length in setover formula.
> - **Formula inversion:** Writing $\tan(\theta) = 2L/(D-d)$ instead of the correct form.
> - **Missing diagrams:** Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if no schematic is drawn.
> - **Numerical precision:** Round off only at the final step; retain 4–5 decimal places in intermediate steps.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q (a)** Explain the **Compound Slide Method** of taper turning with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A taper of **80 mm length** is to be turned on a mild steel workpiece. The diameter at one end is **35 mm** and at the other end is **25 mm**. Calculate:
1. The taper in mm/mm
2. The half-angle of taper
3. The included angle of taper
4. The angle to which the compound slide must be set.

**(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Compound Slide Method**

The compound slide (top slide) is a graduated swivel-mounted slide fitted on top of the cross-slide of the lathe carriage. It can be rotated about a vertical axis and locked at any desired angle between 0° and 90°.

**Working principle:**
1. The compound slide is swiveled by an angle $\theta$ from the cross-slide axis (i.e., from a position perpendicular to the lathe axis).
2. The tool mounted on the tool post is then fed along the axis of the compound slide using the compound slide handwheel.
3. Because the feed direction is now inclined to the workpiece axis by angle $\theta$, the tool cuts a surface inclined at $\theta$ to the axis — producing a taper.

**Setting equation:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

**Advantages:** Suitable for both internal and external tapers; high accuracy.

**Limitations:** Limited length of traverse (≈100 mm); slow production rate (manual feed only).

**Sketch (described in words):** Show the lathe carriage with cross-slide at the base, compound slide swiveled at angle $\theta$ on top, and the tool post with cutting tool at the end. Draw the workpiece axis horizontal and the tool feed direction inclined.

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $D = 35$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $L = 80$ mm.

**(i) Taper:**

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{35 - 25}{80} = \frac{10}{80} = 0.125 \text{ mm/mm}
$$

**(ii) Half-angle of taper:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{2 \times 80} = \frac{10}{160} = 0.0625
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.0625) = 3.576^\circ = 3^\circ 34' 35''
$$

**(iii) Included angle:**

$$
\alpha = 2\theta = 2 \times 3.576^\circ = 7.152^\circ = 7^\circ 09' 10''
$$

**(iv) Compound slide setting angle:**

The compound slide is set to **3° 34′ 35″** (approximately 3° 35′).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q (a)** Explain the **Tailstock Set-Over Method** of taper turning with a neat sketch. Derive the formula for setover. **(7 Marks)**

**Q (b)** A workpiece of length **200 mm** is to be turned with a taper. The large diameter is **45 mm** and the small diameter is **35 mm**. The taper extends over **180 mm** of the workpiece. Calculate:
1. The taper in mm/mm
2. The half-angle of taper
3. The tailstock setover required
4. The included angle of taper. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a): Tailstock Set-Over Method**

In this method, the **tailstock is displaced laterally** (perpendicular to the lathe bed) by a calculated amount called **setover ($x$)**. This shifts the axis of the workpiece so that it is no longer parallel to the longitudinal carriage travel.

**Working principle:**
1. The workpiece is mounted between centers — one in the chuck and one in the tailstock.
2. The tailstock body is offset sideways by $x$ mm.
3. The cutting tool is fed longitudinally along the lathe axis using the carriage handwheel or power feed.
4. Because the workpiece axis is now inclined to the feed direction by angle $\theta$, the tool generates a tapered surface.

**Derivation of setover formula:**

From geometry, when tailstock is offset by $x$ over a length $L$:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{x}{L}
$$

From the taper definition:

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L}
$$

Equating both:

$$
\frac{x}{L} = \frac{D - d}{2L} \implies x = \frac{D - d}{2} = \frac{L \cdot T}{2}
$$

**Advantages:** Long tapers possible; uses power feed.
**Limitations:** Only external tapers; tailstock center wears unevenly; taper cannot start from the very end of the workpiece.

---

**Part (b): Numerical Solution**

**Given:** $L = 180$ mm (tapered length), $D = 45$ mm, $d = 35$ mm.

**(i) Taper:**

$$
T = \frac{D - d}{L} = \frac{45 - 35}{180} = \frac{10}{180} = 0.0556 \text{ mm/mm}
$$

**(ii) Half-angle:**

$$
\tan(\theta) = \frac{D - d}{2L} = \frac{10}{360} = 0.02778
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.02778) = 1.591^\circ = 1^\circ 35' 28''
$$

**(iii) Tailstock setover:**

Using $x = L \times T / 2$:

$$
x = \frac{180 \times 0.0556}{2} = \frac{10}{2} = 5.0 \text{ mm}
$$

(Equivalently, $x = (D-d)/2 = 10/2 = 5.0$ mm.)

**(iv) Included angle:**

$$
\alpha = 2\theta = 2 \times 1.591^\circ = 3.182^\circ = 3^\circ 10' 54''
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Points**

- **Taper definition:** $T = (D - d) / L$ — change in diameter per unit length.
- **Taper Ratio:** $C = T/2 = (D-d)/(2L)$ — tangent of half-angle.
- **Half-angle formula:** $\tan(\theta) = (D-d)/(2L)$ — used for compound slide and taper attachment.
- **Tailstock setover:** $x = L \cdot T / 2 = (D-d)/2$ — used for long gentle tapers.
- **Included angle:** $\alpha = 2\theta$ — the apex angle of the cone.
- **Form tool method** → limited to tapers < 25 mm length.
- **Compound slide method** → most accurate, but limited traverse length.
- **Tailstock setover method** → longest tapers possible, but only external tapers.
- **Morse taper ratio** → approximately 1:20 (MT1, MT2) — a viva favourite.
- **Always measure with sine bar** → most accurate taper measurement in lab.
- **Unit consistency:** Length in mm → taper in mm/mm; convert to taper per inch only if specified.
- **Round-off rule:** Retain 4–5 decimal places in intermediate steps; round only at final answer.
- **Tool setting:** Tool tip must be exactly at lathe center height (use centre gauge).
- **Spindle speed:** Reduce by ~20% compared to straight turning for a good surface finish.
- **Common standard tapers:** Morse (self-holding), Brown & Sharpe, Jarno, Jacobs (chuck tapers).
- **Always draw a labeled sketch** in Part B answers — fetch 1–2 easy marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Methods:** **"F-C-T-T-C"** → **F**orm tool, **C**ompound slide, **T**ailstock setover, **T**aper attachment, **C**NC.

---

**End of Module Experiment 2 — Taper Turning Notes**
*Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | PCPEL407 | Outcome-Based Education | NEP 2020*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Cutting (Single Start Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Thread Cutting (Single Start Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Thread Cutting** is a turning operation performed on a lathe using a single-point threading tool, in which a helical groove of precise geometry is generated on a cylindrical or conical workpiece by synchronizing the linear feed of the carriage with the rotational speed of the spindle.

A **single-start thread** is one in which only **one continuous helical ridge** is wrapped around the cylindrical surface. For a single-start thread, the **Lead (L)** is numerically equal to the **Pitch (P)**, i.e., $L = P$.

The standard reference profile used in the KTU lab curriculum is the **ISO Metric (V-thread) profile**, characterized by:
- **Flank angle: 60°** (between the two flanks of the thread)
- **Flank inclinations: 30°** on each side of the vertical axis
- **Truncation of crest and root:** equal to $\frac{H}{8}$ for external threads

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of wrapping a triangular-prism-shaped rubber band around a pencil. If you push the rubber band along the axis of the pencil by exactly one band-width every time the pencil completes one full rotation, you get a **helical thread**. 

- The **triangle** is the *thread profile* — its **60° apex angle** matches the ISO metric form.
- How far you push the pencil per rotation is the **pitch (P)**. For a single-start thread, the rubber band takes one full turn to advance by one band-width, so lead = pitch.
- The depth to which the rubber band bites into the pencil is the **thread depth (h)** — this is the radial distance from crest to root.

Imagine the cutting tool as a sharp chisel that must remove a tiny amount of material every time the workpiece rotates, while slowly moving sideways. Doing this repeatedly produces a perfect "screw" surface.

### 1.3 Core Highlight Callouts

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 – Module 2):**
