## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
> What is the function of a form-relieved gear cutter? Why is a set of 8 cutters required to cut all standard spur gears?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **form-relieved gear cutter** is a milling cutter whose profile matches the shape of the space between two adjacent involute gear teeth at a given pitch circle. It is called *form-relieved* because its cutting edges are re-ground on the **flank faces only** (not on the tooth profile face), so the profile is preserved even after repeated sharpening.

A **single cutter cannot accurately cut all tooth counts** because the involute curve's shape depends on the **base circle radius**, which in turn depends on the number of teeth. For fewer teeth, the involute is more curved; for many teeth, it is nearly straight (rack-like). The 8-cutter set covers 8 ranges of teeth so that the error in profile is kept within an acceptable manufacturing tolerance.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> State the formula for indexing movement in a plain dividing head having a 40:1 worm gear ratio. Why is the worm-and-wheel mechanism used in dividing heads?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For a plain dividing head with a **40:1 ratio**, the index crank movement is:

$$N = \dfrac{40}{T} \text{ turns (plus fraction as holes)}$$

The **worm-and-wheel** mechanism is used because:
1. It provides a **high reduction ratio in a compact space** — 40 revolutions of the crank produce only 1 revolution of the spindle.
2. The **self-locking** nature of the worm (typically a single-start worm) prevents the spindle from rotating back due to cutting forces, ensuring **positional accuracy** during machining.
3. The **intermittent indexing** required between tooth slots is easily achieved by simply releasing a clamp, turning the crank, and reclamping.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

> **(a)** A spur gear has **30 teeth** and a module of **2.5 mm**. It is to be cut on a milling machine using a plain dividing head (40:1 ratio) and an index plate with hole circles 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49. Calculate the **indexing movement**, **pitch circle diameter**, **addendum**, **dedendum**, and **full depth of cut**. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** Describe with a neat sketch the **procedure for setting the cutter centre at the height of the gear blank centre** and explain why this alignment is critical. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $T = 30$, $m = 2.5$ mm

**Step 1: Indexing movement**

$$N = \dfrac{40}{T} = \dfrac{40}{30} = \dfrac{4}{3} = 1\tfrac{1}{3} \text{ turns}$$

**Step 2: Choose hole circle**

Denominator is 3. Hole circles divisible by 3: 15, 18, 21, 27, 33, 39. Using the **27-hole circle**:

$$H = \dfrac{1}{3} \times 27 = 9 \text{ holes}$$

**Indexing movement: 1 turn + 9 holes on 27-hole circle**

**Step 3: Pitch circle diameter**

$$D = m \cdot T = 2.5 \times 30 = 75 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Addendum**

$$a = 1 \, m = 2.5 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Dedendum**

$$b = 1.25 \, m = 1.25 \times 2.5 = 3.125 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6: Full depth of cut**

$$h = 2.25 \, m = 2.25 \times 2.5 = 5.625 \text{ mm}$$

---

**Model Solution for Q-A (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Procedure to set cutter centre at blank centre height:**

1. Mount the form cutter on the milling machine arbor.
2. Bring the milling table up so that a **scriber or a wiggler** held in the spindle just touches the **top of the gear blank** (which is clamped in the dividing head between centres).
3. Note the **knee elevation reading** on the graduated dial.
4. **Lower** the milling table by a distance equal to the **blank radius $D_o/2$** so that the cutter's horizontal centre line now coincides with the gear blank's centre line.
5. Verify with a **height gauge** placed on the table and moved up to touch the cutter body and the blank surface — both should register the same height reading.

**Why this alignment is critical:**

- If the cutter is set **above** the blank centre, the tooth profile becomes **asymmetric** (one flank steeper than the other) and the involute form is destroyed, leading to **premature wear, noise, and vibration** in mesh.
- If set **below** the centre, the **root fillet radius** becomes too large and **interference** with the mating pinion can occur.
- KTU examiners specifically check for this alignment — common marks are awarded for using a **wiggler or height gauge** rather than "eyeballing" the height.

---

**Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

> **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **functional components of a plain dividing head** and its working principle. **(7 Marks)**

> **(b)** A spur gear of **48 teeth** and module **4 mm** is to be cut on a milling machine. The available index plate has hole circles of 15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20, 21, 23, 27, 29, 31, 33, 37, 39, 41, 43, 47, 49. Calculate the **indexing movement, pitch circle diameter, outside diameter, and root diameter**. List the **cutter number** to be used from the standard 8-piece set. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Functional Components of a Plain Dividing Head:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DivideHead[Plain Dividing Head Block Diagram]
        SP[Input Spindle holds work or chuck] --> WH[40 to 1 Worm and Wheel]
        WH --> CR[Index Crank rotates manually]
        IP[Index Plate with hole circles] --> CR
        CL[Clamping Lever locks spindle] --> SP
    end
```

**Working Principle:**

1. The **input spindle** of the dividing head is driven by the index crank through a **40:1 worm and wheel** reduction.
2. The work (gear blank) is mounted on the spindle between the **live centre** (on the dividing head side) and the **dead centre** (on the footstock side).
3. To make one full rotation of the spindle, the index crank must make **40 complete turns**.
4. To advance by one tooth space ($\frac{1}{T}$ of a revolution), the crank rotates by $\frac{40}{T}$ turns.
5. If $\frac{40}{T}$ is fractional, the whole-number part is the number of full crank turns, and the fraction is realised by inserting the **index pin** into the appropriate **hole on the index plate** after counting the required holes.
6. The **clamp** is engaged to hold the spindle rigidly during cutting.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $T = 48$, $m = 4$ mm

**Step 1: Indexing movement**

$$N = \dfrac{40}{48} = \dfrac{5}{6} \text{ turns}$$

This is purely fractional, so **whole turns = 0**. Choose a hole circle divisible by 6: **18** (or 24 if available, 30, 36, 42, 48). Use **18-hole circle** (Plate A):

$$H = \dfrac{5}{6} \times 18 = 15 \text{ holes}$$

**Indexing: 0 turns + 15 holes on 18-hole circle**

**Step 2: Pitch circle diameter**

$$D = m \cdot T = 4 \times 48 = 192 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Outside diameter**

$$D_o = m(T + 2) = 4 \times 50 = 200 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4: Root diameter**

$$D_r = m(T - 2.5) = 4 \times 45.5 = 182 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5: Cutter selection**

For $T = 48$ (falls in the range 35 to 54), use **Cutter No. 3**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing full depth $h$ with addendum $a$** — The knee is to be raised by **$h = 2.25 m$**, not $a = 1 m$. This is the **#1 error** flagged in KTU valuation camps.
> 2. **Forgetting the clamping lever** — Many students turn the crank but forget to lock the spindle, causing the blank to slip and ruining the tooth spacing.
> 3. **Wrong hole circle selection** — The denominator of the fraction must exactly divide the chosen hole circle. Picking a non-divisible circle (e.g., using 20-hole circle for fraction $1/3$) is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Cutter mounted upside down** — A form cutter mounted inverted cuts a **mirror image** of the tooth profile. The cutter's **cutting edges must face the work**.
> 5. **Not centring the cutter to the blank** — Causes the involute profile to be skewed. Examiners specifically look for **wiggler/height gauge** usage.
> 6. **Skipping the roughing pass** — A single deep pass overloads the form cutter and produces poor surface finish. A 70% roughing + 30% finishing strategy is expected.
> 7. **Wrong cutter number** — A mismatch between tooth count range and cutter number (e.g., using cutter 1 for a 20-tooth gear) leads to profile error and is heavily penalised.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Indexing formula** $N = \dfrac{40}{T}$ is universal for any plain dividing head with 40:1 ratio.
- [x] **Full depth of cut** $h = 2.25 m$ — this is the **vertical knee setting** in mm.
- [x] **Pitch diameter** $D = mT$, **tip diameter** $D_o = m(T+2)$, **root diameter** $D_r = m(T-2.5)$.
- [x] **8-cutter set** covers tooth counts 12–13, 14–16, 17–20, 21–25, 26–34, 35–54, 55–134, 135–rack.
- [x] **Index plate hole circles** are: 15–20, 21–33, 37–49 (three plates). Fractional index must be expressible as holes on one of these circles.
- [x] **Cutter centre must align** with the **gear blank centre** using a wiggler or height gauge.
- [x] **Procedure order**: prepare blank → mount in dividing head → set cutter → align height → set depth $h$ → rough pass → index → repeat → finish pass → inspect.
- [x] **Spindle speed** $N_c = \dfrac{1000 V}{\pi D_c}$ where $V \approx 20$–$30$ m/min for HSS form cutters on mild steel.
- [x] **Always lock the dividing head spindle** with the clamping lever before cutting each tooth slot.
- [x] **Use cutting fluid** — form cutters have poor chip evacuation and heat dissipation.
- [x] **Verification steps** after cutting: measure tooth depth with a depth micrometer (should equal $2.25m$ ± 0.05 mm), check tooth spacing with a vernier, and verify concentricity of the tooth circle using a dial indicator on the OD.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shaping Rectangular Block
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Shaping Rectangular Block

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
A **shaping machine** is a reciprocating type of machine tool that removes metal from a workpiece in the form of chips by moving a rigidly held single-point cutting tool in a linear forward (cutting) stroke and a return (idle) stroke, while the workpiece is fed intermittently at right angles to the tool motion. The experiment of **"Shaping a Rectangular Block"** is a foundational machine-tool laboratory exercise where a raw rectangular workpiece (typically Mild Steel or Aluminum) is machined on a standard **crank-and-slit or crank-driven shaper** to obtain a finished block of precise length, width, height, and squareness with a specified surface finish (typically **Ra 1.6 µm**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** This experiment tests the student's ability to **select cutting parameters, calculate machining time, mount the workpiece and tool correctly, perform straight cuts, and measure the final dimensions** using vernier calipers and micrometers, in line with CO1 and CO2 of PCPEL407.

### Intuitive Analogy — "The Carpenter's Hand Plane"
Imagine a **carpenter's hand plane** sliding back and forth over a wooden plank:
- The **hand plane (tool)** moves forward, shaving off a thin layer of wood (this is the **cutting stroke**).
- The carpenter pulls the plane back without cutting (this is the **return stroke**).
- After every stroke, the plank is pushed slightly forward by a small amount (this is the **feed**).
- After many strokes, the plank becomes flat and smooth.

A shaping machine does **exactly the same thing**, but mechanically, precisely, and on metal. The "plank" is your **rectangular workpiece**, the "plane" is the **shaper tool**, and the "carpenter" is the **bull gear and crank mechanism** that drives everything.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Industrial Context:** Shapers are classified as **low-production machines** primarily used for producing **flat surfaces, keyways, slots, and dovetails**. The maximum stroke length for standard shapers is typically **up to 1000 mm**, with power ratings between **1.5 kW to 7.5 kW**.

### Physical Constants and Standards Used
- **Standard Cutting Speeds for Mild Steel:** 15–30 m/min
- **Standard Feed per Stroke:** 0.1 – 0.4 mm/stroke
- **Standard Depth of Cut:** 0.5 – 3.0 mm
- **Rake Angle (HSS tool for steel):** 10° – 15°
- **Clearance Angle:** 6° – 8°

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Working Principle — The Bull-Gear and Crank Mechanism
The shaping machine converts **rotary motion of the motor** into the **reciprocating linear motion of the ram** through a **crank-and-slit mechanism** (also called the Whitworth quick-return mechanism). This mechanism is the heart of the shaper and is responsible for the asymmetry between the cutting and return strokes.

**Operational Steps (Bulleted Logic):**

1. **Power Transmission:** The electric motor drives a **bull gear** (large gear) via a belt-pulley arrangement. The bull gear rotates at a constant speed `N` (rpm).
2. **Crank Pin Rotation:** A **crank arm** is fixed on the bull gear with an adjustable **crank pin**. The position of the pin determines the **stroke length** `L`.
3. **Slit and Slide Conversion:** The crank pin engages a **slit** on the **slotted lever (rocker arm)**, which is pivoted at the top. As the pin rotates, the slotted lever oscillates.
4. **Ram Motion:** The other end of the slotted lever is connected to the **ram** through a connecting rod called the **bull wheel slide**. The ram moves **to and fro** (reciprocates) on accurately machined guideways.
5. **Quick-Return Action:** The cutting stroke is at lower speed (more time for cutting), while the return stroke is at higher speed (saves time). The **ratio of cutting time to return time** is given by the quick-return ratio `Q`.
6. **Cross-Slide Feed:** The **table** carrying the workpiece is mounted on a **cross-slide** that moves perpendicular to the ram motion. An **indexing pawl-and-ratchet mechanism** provides **intermittent feed** to the table at the end of each return stroke.
7. **Tool Post & Tool:** The cutting tool is held in a **clapper box** on a swiveling tool post mounted on the ram's face. The clapper box **lifts the tool during the return stroke** to prevent rubbing on the machined surface.

### The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step
- **Why is the return stroke faster?** Because no cutting happens during return — to maximize productivity, this dead motion is accelerated.
- **Why does the clapper box lift the tool?** To prevent the tool flank from rubbing against the freshly cut surface, which would degrade surface finish and wear out the tool.
- **Why is feed given only at the end of the return stroke?** To ensure the tool does not feed while cutting (which would produce an uneven surface).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Quick-Return Ratio | $Q = \dfrac{2\beta}{360 - 2\beta} = \dfrac{T_c}{T_r}$ | dimensionless | $T_c$ = cutting time, $T_r$ = return time |
| 2 | Crank Angle for Cutting | $2\beta = 360 \times \dfrac{Q}{1+Q}$ | degrees | For $Q = 2$, $2\beta = 240°$ |
| 3 | Cutting Velocity (mean) | $V_c = \dfrac{L \cdot (1 + \lambda) \cdot N_{st}}{1000}$ | m/min | $L$ = stroke length (mm), $\lambda$ = clearance, $N_{st}$ = strokes/min |
| 4 | Strokes per Minute | $N_{st} = \dfrac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1 + \lambda)}$ | strokes/min | Direct inverse of cutting velocity formula |
| 5 | Machining Time | $t_m = \dfrac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N_{st}}$ | minutes | $L_{total}$ = length of cut + approach + over-run (mm) |
| 6 | Material Removal Rate | $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V_c$ | mm³/min | $f$ = feed (mm/stroke), $d$ = depth of cut (mm) |
| 7 | Power Required | $P = \dfrac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | kW | $F_c$ = cutting force (N), $\eta$ = mechanical efficiency (typically 0.7–0.85) |
| 8 | Specific Cutting Energy | $u_s = \dfrac{P}{MRR}$ | N·m/mm³ | For Mild Steel: ~2.0 N·m/mm³ |
| 9 | Tool Life (Taylor) | $V_c \cdot T^n = C$ | m/min | $n \approx 0.25$ for HSS, $C$ is a constant |
| 10 | Theoretical Surface Roughness | $R_a = \dfrac{f^2}{8R}$ | µm | $R$ = nose radius of tool (mm) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** In actual workshops, the **shaping time formula ($t_m$)** is critical for **process planning and cost estimation**. The **MRR** determines the productivity, and the **power formula** is used to select the machine motor rating. Modern CNC machining centers have replaced shapers for high-volume production, but **shapers are still preferred for tool-room work, maintenance jobs, and prototype manufacturing** due to their simplicity, low cost, and ability to machine internal flat surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Cutting Velocity in a Shaper

The cutting velocity in a shaper is **not constant** (unlike a lathe or milling cutter) because the ram moves with simple harmonic motion. We derive the **mean cutting velocity** $V_c$.

**Setup:**
- Let $L$ = length of the cutting stroke (mm).
- Let $\lambda$ = a factor accounting for the approach and over-run portions of the stroke where no cutting occurs (typically $\lambda \approx 0.1$ to $0.3$).
- Let $N_{st}$ = number of complete (cutting + return) strokes per minute.

**Step 1:** In one complete revolution of the bull gear, the ram completes one cutting stroke and one return stroke. The **effective cutting length per stroke** is $L \cdot (1 + \lambda)$ (where $1$ accounts for the actual cut, and $\lambda \cdot L$ accounts for clearance/over-travel).

**Step 2:** The number of cutting strokes per minute is exactly equal to $N_{st}$ (one cutting stroke per revolution).

**Step 3:** Therefore, the total cutting length traversed per minute is:
$$L_{min} = L \cdot (1 + \lambda) \cdot N_{st} \quad \text{[mm/min]}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to meters per minute (since cutting velocity is conventionally in m/min):
$$V_c = \frac{L \cdot (1 + \lambda) \cdot N_{st}}{1000} \quad \text{[m/min]}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula** for the mean cutting velocity of a shaper.

---

### Derivation 2: Machining Time for Shaping a Rectangular Surface

**Given:**
- Workpiece length to be machined $= L_w$ (mm)
- Tool approach distance $= L_a$ (mm) — typically 5–15 mm
- Tool over-run distance $= L_o$ (mm) — typically 5–10 mm
- Feed per stroke $= f$ (mm/stroke)
- Number of strokes per minute $= N_{st}$
- Number of passes required $= n_p$ (depends on depth of cut $d$ and total material to be removed $D$)

**Step 1 — Total effective stroke length per pass:**
$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o \quad \text{[mm]}$$

**Step 2 — Number of strokes required to cover the feed distance:**
The tool must travel a total feed distance of $L_w$. Since each stroke advances the workpiece by $f$ mm:
$$N_{strokes,total} = \frac{L_w}{f} \quad \text{[strokes per pass]}$$

**Step 3 — Time per pass (in minutes):**
Since $N_{st}$ strokes occur per minute, time per pass is:
$$t_{pass} = \frac{N_{strokes,total}}{N_{st}} = \frac{L_w}{f \cdot N_{st}} \quad \text{[min/pass]}$$

**Step 4 — Total machining time for all passes:**
Including the effective stroke length (since the tool traverses $L_{total}$ per stroke, not just $L_w$):
$$t_m = \frac{L_{total} \cdot L_w}{f \cdot N_{st} \cdot L_w} \cdot n_p = \frac{L_{total} \cdot n_p}{f \cdot N_{st}} \quad \text{[min]}$$

**Step 5 — Most commonly used KTU form:**
$$t_m = \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N_{st}} \quad \text{[min/pass]}$$

Multiplied by $n_p$ passes, the **total machining time** becomes:
$$T_{total} = n_p \cdot \frac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{f \cdot N_{st}} \quad \text{[min]}$$

---

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A Mild Steel block of dimensions $80 \times 50 \times 30$ mm is to be shaped on a $200$ mm stroke shaper to produce a flat surface of length $80$ mm. The cutting speed is $20$ m/min, feed is $0.2$ mm/stroke, depth of cut is $1$ mm per pass, and the total material to be removed is $3$ mm. Calculate: (a) Number of strokes per minute, (b) Machining time per pass, (c) Total machining time. Assume approach = 10 mm, over-run = 5 mm, and clearance factor $\lambda = 0.2$.

**Given Data:**
- $L = 200$ mm (stroke length of shaper)
- $V_c = 20$ m/min
- $f = 0.2$ mm/stroke
- $d = 1$ mm/pass
- $D = 3$ mm (total material)
- $L_w = 80$ mm
- $L_a = 10$ mm
- $L_o = 5$ mm
- $\lambda = 0.2$

**Part (a): Number of strokes per minute**

From the formula:
$$N_{st} = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1 + \lambda)}$$

Substituting values:
$$N_{st} = \frac{1000 \times 20}{200 \times (1 + 0.2)} = \frac{20000}{240} = 83.33 \approx 83 \text{ strokes/min}$$

**Part (b): Machining time per pass**

$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o = 80 + 10 + 5 = 95 \text{ mm}$$

$$t_{pass} = \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N_{st}} = \frac{95}{0.2 \times 83} = \frac{95}{16.6} = 5.72 \text{ min/pass}$$

**Part (c): Total machining time**

Number of passes required:
$$n_p = \frac{D}{d} = \frac{3}{1} = 3 \text{ passes}$$

$$T_{total} = n_p \cdot t_{pass} = 3 \times 5.72 = 17.16 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answer:** $N_{st} \approx 83$ strokes/min, $t_{pass} \approx 5.72$ min, $T_{total} \approx 17.16$ min.

---

### Analytical Matrix — Selection of Cutting Parameters

| Workpiece Material | Cutting Speed $V_c$ (m/min) | Feed $f$ (mm/stroke) | Depth of Cut $d$ (mm) | Tool Material |
|--------------------|------------------------------|----------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Mild Steel (MS) | 15 – 25 | 0.1 – 0.3 | 0.5 – 2.0 | HSS |
| Cast Iron (CI) | 12 – 18 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 1.0 – 3.0 | HSS / Carbide |
| Aluminum | 30 – 60 | 0.2 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 2.0 | HSS |
| Brass | 25 – 40 | 0.2 – 0.4 | 0.5 – 1.5 | HSS |
| Hardened Steel (HRC > 45) | 5 – 10 | 0.05 – 0.15 | 0.2 – 0.5 | Carbide / Ceramic |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Shaping Machine

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the functional flow of components and their interaction during the shaping of a rectangular block.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Electric Motor] --> P1[Belt and Pulley Drive]
    P1 --> BG1[Bull Gear with Crank Pin]
    BG1 --> SL1[Slotted Lever Rocker Arm]
    SL1 --> CR1[Connecting Rod to Ram Slide]
    CR1 --> R1[Ram with Tool Post]
    R1 --> TB1[Clapper Box with Cutting Tool]
    TB1 --> WP1[Workpiece Mounted on Table]
    WP1 --> TS1[Cross Slide with Table]
    TS1 --> FD1[Pawl and Ratchet Feed Mechanism]
    FD1 --> BG1
    BG1 --> QS1[Quick Return Action Controller]
    QS1 --> R1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Power flows from the motor → bull gear → slotted lever → ram → tool → workpiece. Feedback from the bull gear drives the **feed mechanism** which moves the cross-slide (table) intermittently.

---

### Diagram 2: Experimental Workflow for Shaping a Rectangular Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Study Workpiece Drawing] --> A2[Step 2: Select Cutting Parameters]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Mount Workpiece in Vice on Table]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Mount and Grind Shaper Tool]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Set Stroke Length and Position]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Set Feed and Depth of Cut]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Run Machine and Perform Trial Cut]
    A7 --> A8{Measurement OK?}
    A8 -- No --> A9[Adjust Parameters] --> A7
    A8 -- Yes --> A10[Complete All Passes]
    A10 --> A11[Measure Final Dimensions]
    A11 --> A12[Check Surface Finish]
    A12 --> A13[Record Observations and Tabulate]
```

---

### Diagram 3: Crank-and-Slit Mechanism (Simplified Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BG2[Bull Gear Rotating CW] --> CP1[Crank Pin at Radius R]
    CP1 --> SL2[Slit in Slotted Lever]
    SL2 --> LV1[Slotted Lever Pivoted at Top]
    LV1 --> RM1[Ram Connected at Bottom of Lever]
    RM1 --> RT1[Ram Slides on Guideways]
    RT1 --> CL1[Cutting Stroke Direction]
    CL1 --> RR1[Return Stroke Direction]
    RR1 --> BG2
```

**Mechanism Logic:** As the crank pin rotates, the slit slides along the pin, causing the lever to oscillate. The pivot at the top amplifies the motion into the ram's linear reciprocation. The asymmetry of the crank angles for the forward and return paths creates the **quick-return effect**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
*Define a shaping machine. What is the function of a clapper box in a shaper?*

**Model Answer:**
A shaping machine is a reciprocating machine tool used to produce flat surfaces, slots, and keyways by moving a single-point cutting tool in a linear path against a stationary or intermittently fed workpiece. The **clapper box** is a hinged tool holder mounted on the ram that allows the cutting tool to **lift up during the return stroke**, preventing the tool flank from rubbing against the freshly machined surface. This protects the surface finish and extends tool life. (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is the "quick-return ratio" of a shaping machine? If the cutting stroke angle is 240°, calculate the quick-return ratio.*

**Model Answer:**
The quick-return ratio $Q$ is defined as the ratio of the time taken for the cutting stroke to the time taken for the return stroke. It indicates the **time saved** by accelerating the non-cutting return motion.

$$Q = \frac{2\beta}{360 - 2\beta} = \frac{240°}{360° - 240°} = \frac{240}{120} = 2$$

Therefore, the return stroke is **twice as fast** as the cutting stroke. (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse the cutting velocity formula of a shaper with that of a lathe. In a shaper, $V_c$ is a **mean velocity** and depends on **stroke length**, **clearance factor**, and **strokes per minute** — not on diameter.
> - **Always** include the **approach and over-run distances** when calculating machining time, otherwise you will lose **2 to 3 marks**.
> - **Unit consistency** is critical: convert mm to m (divide by 1000) when computing $V_c$ in m/min.
> - **Stroke length must be ≥ workpiece length**; state this assumption clearly in the answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
A rectangular Mild Steel block of dimensions $100 \text{ mm} \times 60 \text{ mm} \times 40 \text{ mm}$ is to be shaped on a standard shaper with a maximum stroke length of $250$ mm. The cutting speed is $18$ m/min, feed is $0.25$ mm/stroke, and depth of cut is $0.8$ mm per pass. Total material to be removed from the top face is $2.4$ mm. The approach and over-run distances are $12$ mm and $8$ mm respectively. Take clearance factor $\lambda = 0.15$.

**(a)** Calculate the number of strokes per minute and the cutting time per pass. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the cutting force is $1200$ N and mechanical efficiency is $80\%$, calculate the power required and the material removal rate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Given: $L = 250$ mm, $V_c = 18$ m/min, $f = 0.25$ mm/stroke, $d = 0.8$ mm, $D = 2.4$ mm, $L_w = 100$ mm, $L_a = 12$ mm, $L_o = 8$ mm, $\lambda = 0.15$.

Number of strokes per minute:
$$N_{st} = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1 + \lambda)} = \frac{1000 \times 18}{250 \times 1.15} = \frac{18000}{287.5} = 62.6 \text{ strokes/min}$$

Total effective length per pass:
$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o = 100 + 12 + 8 = 120 \text{ mm}$$

Cutting time per pass:
$$t_{pass} = \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N_{st}} = \frac{120}{0.25 \times 62.6} = \frac{120}{15.65} = 7.67 \text{ min/pass}$$

**Part (b):**
Cutting force $F_c = 1200$ N, efficiency $\eta = 0.8$.

Cutting velocity (mean):
$$V_c = 18 \text{ m/min} = \frac{18}{60} = 0.3 \text{ m/s}$$

Power required:
$$P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{\eta} = \frac{1200 \times 0.3}{0.8} = 450 \text{ W} = 0.45 \text{ kW}$$

Material Removal Rate:
$$MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V_c = 0.25 \times 0.8 \times 18000 = 3600 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$$

(Here, $V_c$ is used in mm/min: $18$ m/min $= 18000$ mm/min)

**Final Answer (A):** $N_{st} \approx 63$ strokes/min, $t_{pass} \approx 7.67$ min, $P = 0.45$ kW, $MRR = 3600$ mm³/min.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice**

**Q. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working principle of a crank-and-slit quick-return mechanism used in a shaping machine. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A shaper is used to machine a flat surface of length $150$ mm on a Cast Iron workpiece. The stroke length is $300$ mm, cutting speed is $15$ m/min, feed is $0.3$ mm/stroke, depth of cut is $1.5$ mm, and clearance factor is $0.2$. The approach and over-run distances are $10$ mm each. Calculate: (i) Number of strokes per minute, (ii) Total machining time for 2 passes. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Refer to **Section 2, Working Principle** and **Diagram 3** in Section 4 for the full explanation. Key points to be included:

1. Motor drives the **bull gear** via belt-pulley.
2. Bull gear carries an adjustable **crank pin** at radius $R$ (sets stroke length).
3. Crank pin engages a **slit** in a **slotted lever** pivoted at the top.
4. As the pin rotates, the slit slides, oscillating the lever.
5. The lower end of the lever is connected to the **ram** via a connecting rod.
6. The ram moves **to and fro** on V-flat guideways.
7. The crank angle for the cutting stroke ($2\beta$) is **greater than 180°**, making the return stroke **faster** (quick-return).
8. The quick-return ratio $Q = \frac{2\beta}{360 - 2\beta}$ is typically between 1.5 and 3.

(7 marks — include labeled diagram for full marks)

**Part (b):**
Given: $L = 300$ mm, $V_c = 15$ m/min, $f = 0.3$ mm/stroke, $d = 1.5$ mm, $\lambda = 0.2$, $L_w = 150$ mm, $L_a = L_o = 10$ mm, $n_p = 2$.

(i) Number of strokes per minute:
$$N_{st} = \frac{1000 \cdot V_c}{L \cdot (1 + \lambda)} = \frac{1000 \times 15}{300 \times 1.2} = \frac{15000}{360} = 41.67 \approx 42 \text{ strokes/min}$$

(ii) Total effective length per pass:
$$L_{total} = L_w + L_a + L_o = 150 + 10 + 10 = 170 \text{ mm}$$

Time per pass:
$$t_{pass} = \frac{L_{total}}{f \cdot N_{st}} = \frac{170}{0.3 \times 41.67} = \frac{170}{12.5} = 13.6 \text{ min/pass}$$

Total machining time for 2 passes:
$$T_{total} = n_p \cdot t_{pass} = 2 \times 13.6 = 27.2 \text{ min}$$

**Final Answer (B):** $N_{st} \approx 42$ strokes/min, $T_{total} = 27.2$ min.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **shaper** uses **reciprocating linear motion** of a single-point tool to produce flat surfaces.
- The **crank-and-slit mechanism** converts rotary motion to reciprocating motion with a **quick-return ratio** typically between **1.5 and 3**.
- The **clapper box** lifts the tool during the return stroke to protect the machined surface.
- **Cutting velocity formula (KTU high-yield):** $V_c = \frac{L \cdot (1 + \lambda) \cdot N_{st}}{1000}$ m/min
- **Machining time formula (KTU high-yield):** $t_m = \frac{L_w + L_a + L_o}{f \cdot N_{st}}$ min/pass
- **MRR formula:** $MRR = f \cdot d \cdot V_c$ mm³/min
- **Power formula:** $P = \frac{F_c \cdot V_c}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ kW
- **Always** convert $V_c$ to mm/min when using the MRR formula (multiply by 1000).
- **Always** include approach and over-run distances in $L_{total}$ — examiners strictly check this.
- For **Mild Steel**, use HSS tool with **10°–15° rake angle** and **6°–8° clearance angle**.
- **Number of passes** $n_p = \frac{\text{Total material to remove}}{\text{Depth of cut per pass}} = \frac{D}{d}$.
- **Workpiece must be gripped in a machine vice** with the surface to be machined parallel to the ram's line of motion.
- **Safety:** Always use a **tool guard**, keep hands away from the cutting zone, and ensure proper **work-holding** before starting the machine.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular Slot and Dovetail Cutting – Part I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Rectangular Slot and Dovetail Cutting – Part I

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Rectangular Slot – Formal Definition

A **rectangular slot** is a closed or open groove machined into a workpiece having a **flat bottom, two vertical (90°) side walls, and a uniform width** equal to the diameter of the milling cutter used. It is one of the most fundamental slotting operations carried out on a **horizontal or vertical milling machine** using a plain slab milling cutter, side-and-face milling cutter, or an end mill cutter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL407 – Module 2):** The student must be able to *plan the machining sequence, select the cutter, compute cutting parameters, and perform the slot/dovetail cutting operation on a given workpiece using a milling machine.*

## 1.2 Dovetail – Formal Definition

A **dovetail** is a trapezoidal-shaped male or female profile (resembling the tail of a dove) machined on a workpiece. It is characterised by an **incline angle** (commonly **45° or 60°**) on its sides. The dovetail provides a **positive locking action** against sliding in the direction of the pull-out force and is widely used for:

- Slides of machine tools (lathe bed, milling table, etc.)
- Tool holders and fixture locating surfaces
- Joining mating components in jigs and dies

A **dovetail slot (female)** is the groove cut into a component, while a **dovetail tail (male)** is the matching projecting rib.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - **Rectangular Slot** → Think of the *channel carved in a wooden door frame* that holds a sliding panel. The groove has straight, vertical walls; the slider simply glides in and out.
> - **Dovetail Joint** → Think of the *interlocking wooden joint used in fine carpentry and drawer construction*. The wider, flared shape of the male piece physically prevents it from being pulled out vertically — a pure mechanical interlock requiring no screws or glue.
> - In a machine tool, the **dovetail slide of a lathe cross-slide** works exactly the same way — the wide base of the slide sits inside the dovetail slot, ensuring positive location and resistance to lifting.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics

| Term | Symbol | Standard Value (Mild Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Cutting Speed | V | **20–30 m/min** (HSS cutter) |
| Feed per tooth | fz | **0.05–0.15 mm/tooth** |
| Depth of cut | d | 2–8 mm (roughing) |
| Cutter material | – | HSS / Carbide |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic for Rectangular Slot Cutting

The operation proceeds in a strict logical sequence:

1. **Workholding** – The workpiece is clamped on the milling machine table using a machine vice or T-bolts, with the surface to be machined parallel to the table.
2. **Cutter Selection** – For a through rectangular slot, the cutter diameter must equal the slot width. A plain milling cutter (for horizontal milling) or an end mill (for vertical milling) is used.
3. **Alignment** – The cutter is positioned directly above the marked slot line using a wiggler / edge finder.
4. **Parameter Setting** – Spindle speed (N) and table feed (f) are calculated and set.
5. **Cutting Passes** – A series of plunge cuts along the slot length, with a pre-set depth of cut adjusted per pass using the knee (vertical feed).
6. **Inspection** – Width measured with a vernier, depth with a depth micrometer, surface finish with a surface comparator.

## 2.2 Operational Logic for Dovetail Cutting

A dovetail can be cut in two ways:

| Method | Cutter Used | Machine Setup | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Using Dovetail Cutter** | Single-angle cutter (45°/60°) | Vertical Milling Machine (VMC) | Small dovetails, tool holders |
| **Using Angular Cutter + Vertical Slide** | Plain milling cutter + tilting vice | Horizontal Milling Machine | Large dovetails (machine tool slides) |

## 2.3 The Core "Why" Behind the Geometry

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the dovetail sloped?**
> A rectangular slot relies on friction, fasteners, or shoulders to resist vertical pull-out. A **dovetail's outward-flaring sides** convert any vertical pull-out force into a *wedging compression* on the angled walls. The wider the bottom (or top) and the steeper the angle, the stronger the interlock — but the harder it is to assemble/disassemble.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ | Spindle speed | RPM |
| 2 | $f_{table} = f_z \times z \times N$ | Table feed rate | mm/min |
| 3 | $t_c = \dfrac{L}{f_{table}}$ | Cutting time | min |
| 4 | $MRR = w \times d \times f_{table}$ | Material removal rate | mm³/min |
| 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \times k_c}{60 \times 10^6 \times \eta}$ | Cutting power (rough estimate) | kW |
| 6 | $W_{top} = W_{bottom} + 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$ | Dovetail top width | mm |
| 7 | $W_{bottom} = W_{top} - 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$ | Dovetail bottom width | mm |

Where:
- $V$ = cutting speed (m/min)
- $D$ = cutter diameter (mm)
- $f_z$ = feed per tooth (mm/tooth)
- $z$ = number of teeth on cutter
- $L$ = length of cut (mm)
- $w$ = width of cut (mm)
- $d$ = depth of cut (mm)
- $k_c$ = specific cutting force (N/mm²)
- $\eta$ = machine efficiency
- $\alpha$ = dovetail angle (45° or 60°)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in **CAM programming, process planning, fixture design, and CNC verification** in industries like aerospace (wing rib slots), automotive (engine block dovetails), and heavy machinery (lathe/milling bed slides).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Spindle Speed for Slot Cutting

**Given:** Mild steel workpiece, plain milling cutter diameter $D = 60 \text{ mm}$, recommended cutting speed $V = 25 \text{ m/min}$.

**Step 1 – Write down the governing kinematic relationship.**

The cutting speed is the tangential velocity at the periphery of the rotating cutter:

$$V = \pi \times D \times N \quad \text{(in mm/min if D in mm, N in RPM)}$$

**Step 2 – Convert to standard SI form.**

To obtain $N$ in RPM when $V$ is in m/min, multiply $V$ by 1000 to convert metres to millimetres:

$$V_{mm/min} = V_{m/min} \times 1000$$

**Step 3 – Rearrange the kinematic equation to solve for N.**

$$N = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$$

**Step 4 – Substitute the numerical values.**

$$N = \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 60} = \dfrac{25000}{188.496}$$

$$N = 132.6 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 5 – Round to the nearest standard spindle speed available on the machine.**

$$N_{selected} = 140 \text{ RPM} \quad \text{(next higher standard step)}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Table Feed for Slot Cutting

**Given:** Plain milling cutter with $z = 8$ teeth, $f_z = 0.10 \text{ mm/tooth}$, $N = 140 \text{ RPM}$.

**Step 1 – The table feed depends on how many teeth engage the workpiece per minute.**

Each tooth removes one chip per revolution; with $N$ revolutions per minute and $z$ teeth:

$$\text{Chips per minute} = z \times N$$

**Step 2 – Each chip carries feed per tooth, $f_z$.**

Therefore the total linear travel of the table per minute is:

$$f_{table} = f_z \times z \times N$$

**Step 3 – Substitute values.**

$$f_{table} = 0.10 \times 8 \times 140 = 112 \text{ mm/min}$$

**Step 4 – Select a practical feed from the machine's feed dial.**

The closest available feed (typical milling machine dial): $f_{selected} = 112 \text{ mm/min}$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Cutting Time

**Given:** Slot length $L = 120 \text{ mm}$, $f_{table} = 112 \text{ mm/min}$.

**Step 1 – The cutting time is the time taken for the table to traverse the entire slot length once.**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L}{f_{table}}$$

**Step 2 – Substitute values.**

$$t_c = \dfrac{120}{112} = 1.071 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 – Convert to seconds for KTU practical record entry.**

$$t_c = 1.071 \times 60 = 64.3 \text{ seconds}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Dovetail Geometry Calculation

**Given:** A **45° dovetail** with a slot bottom width $W_b = 20 \text{ mm}$ and depth $d = 15 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 – Recall the geometric relationship.**

For a dovetail, the **top width is always larger than the bottom width** (in a female slot) by an amount determined by the angle and depth.

$$W_{top} = W_{bottom} + 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$$

**Step 2 – Substitute $\alpha = 45°$ and evaluate $\tan(\alpha/2)$.**

$$\tan(22.5°) = 0.4142$$

**Step 3 – Substitute numerical values.**

$$W_{top} = 20 + 2 \times 15 \times 0.4142$$

$$W_{top} = 20 + 30 \times 0.4142$$

$$W_{top} = 20 + 12.426 = 32.426 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4 – For a 60° dovetail with same dimensions, recompute.**

$$\tan(30°) = 0.5774$$

$$W_{top,60} = 20 + 2 \times 15 \times 0.5774 = 20 + 17.32 = 37.32 \text{ mm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** A **steeper dovetail angle (60°)** produces a **larger top width** for the same depth — providing a *stronger mechanical interlock* but requiring *more material removal* and *higher machining time*.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Cutter Selection Decision Table

| Workpiece Material | Operation | Recommended Cutter | V (m/min) | fz (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mild Steel | Rectangular Slot (open) | Plain milling cutter | 20–25 | 0.08–0.12 |
| Mild Steel | Rectangular Slot (closed) | End mill | 25–30 | 0.05–0.10 |
| Cast Iron | Rectangular Slot | Carbide end mill | 60–80 | 0.10–0.15 |
| Aluminium | Rectangular Slot | HSS end mill (2-flute) | 100–150 | 0.05–0.10 |
| Mild Steel | Dovetail (45°) | 45° dovetail cutter | 20–25 | 0.05–0.08 |
| Mild Steel | Dovetail (60°) | 60° dovetail cutter | 20–25 | 0.05–0.08 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Rectangular Slot – Workpiece & Cutter Setup (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Workpiece Mild Steel Block] --> B[Layout Marking with Scriber and Surface Gauge]
    B --> C[Clamping in Machine Vice on Milling Table]

```

    B --> C[Clamp on Tilting Vice set to Required Dove Angle]

    D -- Yes --> E[Mount Dove Cutter on Vertical Spindle]

    F --> G
    G --> H[Plunge Cut to Full Depth d]

## 4.3 Geometric Profile Comparison (Block Schematic)

## 4.4 Physical Schematic Description (for record drawing)

When drawing the experimental setup in your KTU lab record, depict the following:

- **Rectangular Slot Setup:** Side view of the milling machine table with a vice holding the workpiece. Show the end mill / plain cutter above the slot. Indicate **cutting direction (down milling preferred)**, **feed direction**, and **depth of cut (d)**.
- **Dovetail Slot Setup:** Show the dovetail cutter (or plain cutter with tilting vice) producing an angled wall on the workpiece. Mark the **angle α** at the side wall, **bottom width $W_b$**, **top width $W_t$**, and **depth $d$**.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]`
**List any three applications of a dovetail joint in machine tool construction.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Lathe cross-slide and compound slide** – the dovetail ensures the slide remains flat on the bed and resists lifting forces during cutting.
2. **Milling machine knee and table slides** – the dovetail supports vertical and longitudinal loads while allowing accurate sliding motion.
3. **Drill press quill and column ways** – the dovetail provides rigid vertical guidance for the spindle.
4. *(Bonus)* Tool post slots on shapers and planers.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]`
**Differentiate between a plain milling cutter and a dovetail cutter.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Plain Milling Cutter | Dovetail Cutter |
|---|---|---|
| Shape | Cylindrical, peripheral teeth only | Cylindrical body with **angled teeth on the periphery** |
| Profile Produced | Flat surface / rectangular slot | Trapezoidal dovetail profile |
| Angled Sides | No (all teeth parallel to axis) | Yes (cutting edges at 45°/60° to axis) |
| Application | Horizontal milling of flat surfaces | Dovetail slots in a single pass |
| Mounting | Horizontal spindle | Vertical spindle |

---

## Part B Question A (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option A)

### `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Question A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for machining a **rectangular slot** of width **20 mm and depth 10 mm** on a mild steel workpiece using a **plain milling cutter** on a horizontal milling machine. List the cutters, work-holding devices, and measuring instruments used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A plain milling cutter of **diameter 80 mm and 8 teeth** is used to cut a mild steel workpiece. The recommended cutting speed is **25 m/min** and the feed per tooth is **0.10 mm/tooth**. Calculate (i) spindle speed, (ii) table feed, and (iii) cutting time for a **150 mm** long slot. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Apparatus Required:**
- Horizontal milling machine
- Plain milling cutter (Ø 80 mm, HSS)
- Machine vice with parallel strips
- Surface gauge, scriber, hammer, marking table
- Vernier caliper, depth micrometer, surface plate
- Cutting fluid

**Procedure:**
1. **Marking:** The slot centre line and boundaries are scribed on the prepared workpiece surface using a surface gauge and marking medium (dye or chalk).
2. **Work-holding:** The workpiece is gripped in the machine vice with the marked surface parallel to the milling machine table. Parallels are placed under the workpiece to keep the slot clear of the vice jaws.
3. **Cutter mounting:** A plain milling cutter of diameter 80 mm is mounted on the horizontal milling machine arbor and securely tightened.
4. **Alignment:** The cutter is brought above the marked slot line using a dial indicator or by hand-rotating the cutter to a near-touching position. The table is set to the appropriate starting coordinate using the longitudinal hand wheel.
5. **Parameter setting:** Spindle speed and table feed are set per the calculated values.
6. **Cutting:** The knee is raised until the cutter just touches the workpiece. The required depth of cut (10 mm) is set using the knee elevating wheel. A plunge cut is taken, and the table is traversed longitudinally to cover the full 150 mm slot length.
7. **Inspection:** Slot width is checked with a vernier caliper; depth with a depth micrometer; surface roughness with a comparator.
8. **Safety:** Safety glasses, apron, and avoiding loose clothing are mandatory.

---

### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Given:**
- $D = 80 \text{ mm}$
- $z = 8$ teeth
- $V = 25 \text{ m/min}$
- $f_z = 0.10 \text{ mm/tooth}$
- $L = 150 \text{ mm}$

**(i) Spindle Speed (N):**

$$N = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D} = \dfrac{25 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80}$$

$$N = \dfrac{25000}{251.33} = 99.47 \text{ RPM}$$

$$N_{selected} = 100 \text{ RPM} \text{ (nearest standard speed)}$$

**(ii) Table Feed ($f_{table}$):**

$$f_{table} = f_z \times z \times N = 0.10 \times 8 \times 100 = 80 \text{ mm/min}$$

**(iii) Cutting Time ($t_c$):**

$$t_c = \dfrac{L}{f_{table}} = \dfrac{150}{80} = 1.875 \text{ min}$$

$$\boxed{t_c = 1.875 \text{ min} \approx 1 \text{ min } 52 \text{ sec}}$$

---

## Part B Question B (14 Marks – Internal Choice Option B)

### `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**Question B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Explain with a sketch how a **dovetail slot of 45° angle, 20 mm bottom width, and 15 mm depth** is cut on a vertical milling machine. Derive the **top width of the slot**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List **four differences** between machining a **rectangular slot** and a **dovetail slot**. State **two industrial applications** of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Setup:** The workpiece is clamped on the vertical milling machine table. A **45° dovetail cutter** (single-angle milling cutter) is mounted on the vertical spindle.

**Procedure:**
1. Layout: Centre line and both angled side lines (at 22.5° from vertical) are marked.
2. The dovetail cutter is aligned over the slot centre using an edge finder.
3. Plunge cut is taken to the full depth of 15 mm.
4. Longitudinal feed is applied to traverse the full length of the slot.
5. Inspection: The dovetail angle is verified using a **bevel protractor** or a **dovetail gauge**; widths are measured with vernier calipers.

**Derivation of Top Width:**

$$W_{top} = W_{bottom} + 2 d \tan(\alpha / 2)$$

$$W_{top} = 20 + 2 \times 15 \times \tan(22.5°)$$

$$W_{top} = 20 + 30 \times 0.4142 = 20 + 12.426$$

$$\boxed{W_{top} = 32.43 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

| # | Rectangular Slot | Dovetail Slot |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plain / end mill cutter | Angled dovetail cutter |
| 2 | Vertical walls at 90° | Angled walls (45°/60°) |
| 3 | Width = cutter diameter | Width depends on angle and depth |
| 4 | No mechanical interlock | Provides positive mechanical interlock |
| 5 | Easier to machine and inspect | Requires dovetail gauges for inspection |

**Applications:**
- **Rectangular slot:** T-slot in milling machine table, keyway in shafts, channel for sliding panels.
- **Dovetail slot:** Lathe cross-slide ways, milling machine knee slide, machine tool guideways.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** in cutting speed formula — $V$ is in m/min but $D$ is in mm. **Always multiply V by 1000** in the numerator.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert the spindle speed** result to the nearest standard speed of the machine. If the calculated value is 99.47 RPM, writing "99.47 RPM" without selecting 100 RPM loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing bottom width and top width** in dovetail problems. The **smaller width is always the BOTTOM** of a female dovetail slot.
> 4. **Mixing up $\alpha$ and $\alpha/2$** in the dovetail formula. The full angle $\alpha$ is the total included angle; the trigonometric term uses **half angle**.
> 5. **Omitting the sketch** in the procedure question — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks specifically for the labelled diagram. Always include a side-view sketch of the cutter-workpiece-table setup.
> 6. **Not stating the cutter material** (HSS / Carbide) — examiners expect it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Rectangular slot** width = cutter diameter; depth is set by the knee wheel.
- **Dovetail slot** width varies with depth; the formula $W_{top} = W_{bottom} + 2 d \tan(\alpha/2)$ is **must-know**.
- The **two standard dovetail angles** are **45°** (tool holders, jigs) and **60°** (heavy machine tool slides).
- The **spindle speed formula** $N = \dfrac{V \times 1000}{\pi \times D}$ is **mandatory** — write units explicitly.
- The **table feed formula** $f_{table} = f_z \times z \times N$ requires **three parameters** — never skip one.
- **Cutting time** is **length / feed** for milling — a frequent KTU 2-mark sub-question.
- **Work-holding device** for milling is the **machine vice with parallels**; the workpiece should never be clamped directly on the table for slot cutting.
- **Inspection tools:** Vernier caliper (width), depth micrometer (depth), bevel protractor / dovetail gauge (dovetail angle).
- **Safety:** Always use safety glasses, avoid loose clothing, and never reach over a rotating cutter.
- **Down milling (climb milling)** is preferred for rectangular slots to get a better surface finish.
- **Dovetail cutters** cut in a single pass only if the diameter is greater than the **top width** of the slot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rectangular Slot and Dovetail Cutting – Part II
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Rectangular Slot and Dovetail Cutting – Part II

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Dovetail

A **dovetail** is a wedge-shaped interlocking joint or feature machined into a workpiece, characterized by a flared, trapezoidal cross-section. The male (external) dovetail slides into the matching female (internal) dovetail, providing exceptional resistance against pulling apart in the direction perpendicular to the slide.

In the context of the **Shaper Machine** experiment, a dovetail is produced by angularly cutting the sides of a workpiece at a specified included angle (commonly **45°**, **60°**, or **30°**) using a form tool or by swiveling the toolhead and vice.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCPEL407):** Dovetail cutting is a shaping operation where a workpiece is machined to produce a trapezoidal slot or projection that resists axial separation. The operation is performed on a shaper using either a form tool, an angularly set vice, or by swiveling the tool slide.

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy — The "Wedge That Never Lets Go"

Imagine two wooden toy blocks. The top one is shaped like a wedge pointing downward, and the bottom one has a matching flared slot. When you push them together, they lock firmly and cannot be pulled straight apart — the **angled walls** jam against each other and mechanically resist separation. 

This is the same principle used in:
- **Slide rests** of machine tools (the cross-slide gib)
- **Tool holders** on milling machines
- **Drawer joints** in fine furniture
- **Bicycle seat post clamps**

In all these cases, the **angled taper generates a self-locking mechanical interlock** without the need for fasteners.

## 1.3 Standard Dovetail Parameters

The fundamental geometric parameters of a dovetail, expressed in standard SI units (**millimetres**), are:

- **W** = Width of the dovetail at the **top (open) end**, in **mm**
- **w** = Width of the dovetail at the **base (root) end**, in **mm**
- **α** = Included angle of the dovetail, in **degrees (°)**
- **h** = Depth of the dovetail (axial), in **mm**
- **L** = Length of the dovetail, in **mm**
- **C** = Side clearance angle provided on the cutting tool, in **degrees (°)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** For a male (external) dovetail, **W > w** (flared outward). For a female (internal) dovetail, **W < w** (flared inward) when measured from the same reference plane.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Dovetails (Engineering Classification)

The classification is based on the included angle and the relative dimensions:

| Type of Dovetail | Included Angle (α) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Full Dovetail** | **45°** | Tool holders, slide gibs, machine tool beds |
| **Half Dovetail** | **30°** | Light-load interlocking applications |
| **Steep Dovetail** | **60°** | Heavy load transfer, high retention force |
| **Through Dovetail** | Variable | Passes completely through the mating part |
| **Blind Dovetail** | Variable | Stops at a defined depth (requires relief at the end) |
| **Male (External) Dovetail** | Variable | Projecting wedge-shaped feature |
| **Female (Internal) Dovetail** | Variable | Recessed wedge-shaped slot |

## 2.2 Working Principle of Dovetail Cutting on a Shaper

The shaping of a dovetail involves the following physical mechanisms:

1. **Linear Reciprocation** — The shaper ram carries the cutting tool in a straight-line forward (cutting) stroke and a return (idle) stroke at a constant stroke length.
2. **Cross-Feed** — After each double stroke, the tool (or table) is fed perpendicular to the cutting direction by a small increment (typically **0.1 mm to 0.5 mm per stroke** for roughing, **0.05 mm to 0.1 mm** for finishing).
3. **Angular Cutting** — The dovetail's sloped side is generated by either:
   - **Method A:** Swiveling the **tool head** to the half-angle (α/2) of the dovetail, OR
   - **Method B:** Swiveling the **machine vice** to the half-angle and feeding the tool vertically.
4. **Clearance Provision** — The tool is ground with a **side clearance angle (C ≈ 3° to 8°)** on the trailing flank to prevent rubbing on the freshly cut angled surface.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are **must-know** for the University Exam and lab record:

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{D - w}{2h}$ | Relation between depth, top/bottom width, and included angle for an **internal (female)** dovetail | **mm**, **degrees** |
| $\tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \dfrac{W - w}{2h}$ | Relation for an **external (male)** dovetail | **mm**, **degrees** |
| $D = w + 2h \tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Top width of an **internal (female)** dovetail | **mm** |
| $W = w + 2h \tan\left(\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\right)$ | Top width of an **external (male)** dovetail | **mm** |
| $h = \dfrac{D - w}{2\tan(\alpha/2)}$ | Depth of dovetail when width difference is given | **mm** |
| $\theta_{tool} = 90° - \dfrac{\alpha}{2} - C$ | Rake angle of the form tool (for proper cutting action) | **degrees** |
| $v_c = \dfrac{\pi D N}{1000}$ | Cutting speed for the shaper, where **D** is workpiece length equivalent and **N** is stroke frequency | **m/min** |
| $f_r = s \times f$ | Feed rate = stroke length × feed per stroke | **mm/min** |
| $T_m = \dfrac{L \times n}{f}$ | Machining time (approximate) for shaper operation | **min** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Notation Warning:** In the KTU standard, the symbol **D** denotes the **diameter of the bore** (for internal dovetail) while **W** denotes the **width at the open end of the dovetail** (for external dovetail). The bottom width is universally **w**. Always confirm the convention used in your question paper.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Dovetails are indispensable in:

- **Machine Tool Slideways** — The cross-slide and vertical slide of a lathe or milling machine are guided by a dovetail gib, ensuring **zero backlash** and **high stiffness**.
- **Modular Tooling** — Quick-change tool posts on lathes use dovetail interlocks.
- **Fixtures and Jigs** — Dovetail clamps hold workpieces without external fasteners.
- **Aerospace Assemblies** — Dovetail joints appear in turbine blade mounting and composite panel assembly.

The engineering insight: a dovetail converts **axial force into a normal force** at the angled walls, producing massive **frictional resistance** proportional to the cotangent of the half-angle. The smaller the included angle, the stronger the self-locking action.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relationship Between Depth, Width, and Included Angle

**Statement to prove:** For an internal (female) dovetail, the depth **h**, top width **D**, bottom width **w**, and included angle **α** are related by:

$$D = w + 2h \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

Consider the cross-section of an internal dovetail cut into a rectangular block. The dovetail is a trapezoidal slot whose two sloped sides meet at an included angle **α** at the bottom (root) of the slot.

**Step 1 — Symmetry Argument:**
By the symmetry of a standard dovetail about its vertical centre line, each sloped wall makes an angle of **α/2** with the vertical centre line.

**Step 2 — Define the Geometry:**
Let **h** = vertical depth of the dovetail from the open top surface to the root.
Let **w** = width of the dovetail at the root (bottom).
Let **D** = width of the dovetail at the top (open surface).

**Step 3 — Horizontal Projection of One Sloped Wall:**
Consider the right-hand sloped wall. It rises from a point at horizontal distance **w/2** from the centre line at the root, to a point at horizontal distance **D/2** from the centre line at the top.

The horizontal distance covered by this one wall is:

$$\Delta x = \frac{D}{2} - \frac{w}{2} = \frac{D - w}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Trigonometry:**
The sloped wall rises vertically by a height **h** and runs horizontally by **Δx**. The angle this wall makes with the vertical is **α/2**. Therefore:

$$\tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{\text{Opposite (horizontal)}}{\text{Adjacent (vertical)}} = \frac{\Delta x}{h} = \frac{D - w}{2h}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to Solve for D:**

$$\tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = \frac{D - w}{2h}$$

Multiply both sides by **2h**:

$$2h \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) = D - w$$

Add **w** to both sides:

$$D = w + 2h \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Depth from Given Width Difference

**Given:** A male dovetail has a top width **W = 40 mm** and bottom width **w = 24 mm**. The included angle is **45°**. Find the depth **h**.

**Step 1 — Identify the relevant formula:**

$$h = \frac{W - w}{2\tan(\alpha/2)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the half-angle:**

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{45°}{2} = 22.5°$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$h = \frac{40 - 24}{2 \times \tan(22.5°)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the trigonometric term:**

$$\tan(22.5°) = 0.4142$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerator and denominator:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 16 \text{ mm}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 2 \times 0.4142 = 0.8284$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$h = \frac{16}{0.8284} = 19.314 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 19.31 \text{ mm}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameters on Dovetail Geometry

| Parameter Changed | Effect on Top Width (D or W) | Effect on Depth (h) | Self-Locking Strength |
|---|---|---|---|
| **α increases** (e.g., 30° → 60°) | Increases (for fixed **w, h**) | Decreases (for fixed **W, w**) | **Decreases** |
| **α decreases** (e.g., 60° → 30°) | Decreases | Increases | **Increases** |
| **h increases** (for fixed **W, w**, **α**) | — | — | **Increases** (longer lever arm) |
| **w increases** (for fixed **D, h**, **α**) | Decreases (since D fixed) | — | Slight increase |
| **Side clearance C decreases** | — | — | Tool life **decreases** |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Internal Dovetail Calculation

**Problem:** An internal (female) dovetail is to be cut on a shaper. The bore diameter at the open end is **D = 60 mm**, the included angle is **α = 60°**, and the required depth is **h = 25 mm**. Calculate the bottom width **w** of the dovetail.

**Step 1 — Rearrange the master formula for w:**

$$w = D - 2h \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute α/2 = 30°:**

$$w = 60 - 2 \times 25 \times \tan(30°)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate tan(30°):**

$$\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

**Step 4 — Compute the product term:**

$$2 \times 25 \times 0.5774 = 28.868 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Final subtraction:**

$$w = 60 - 28.868 = 31.132 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{w \approx 31.13 \text{ mm}}$$

**Verification (KTU Examiner's Trick):** Always check that **w < D** for an internal dovetail. Here, **31.13 < 60**, so the geometry is valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
