# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Define a dovetail. List any three types of dovetails used in engineering practice."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **dovetail** is a wedge-shaped interlocking joint whose cross-section is trapezoidal, with the wider end (top) and narrower end (root) connected by sloped sides at a defined included angle. The wedge geometry provides mechanical resistance to axial separation through frictional and normal contact forces.

**Three types of dovetails (1 mark each):**

1. **Full Dovetail (α = 45°)** — Standard machine tool slideway dovetail.
2. **Half Dovetail (α ≈ 30°)** — Used for lighter duty applications where self-locking must be strong.
3. **Through Dovetail** — Dovetail that passes completely through the mating part.

*(Alternative valid answers: blind dovetail, male/female dovetail, steep dovetail — any 3 accepted.)*

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Why is a side clearance angle provided on a dovetail cutting tool?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **side clearance angle (C)** is provided on the trailing flank of the dovetail cutting tool to prevent the flank from **rubbing against the freshly machined angled surface** of the dovetail during the return stroke and the cutting stroke.

If the clearance is absent, the tool flank drags on the workpiece, causing:
- **Heat generation** and thermal damage to the cutting edge.
- **Rapid tool wear** and reduced tool life.
- **Poor surface finish** on the dovetail walls.

The recommended clearance angle is typically **C = 3° to 8°**, ensuring smooth cutting action while maintaining the tool's wedge geometry.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the procedure for cutting a **45° internal dovetail** on a shaper using a form tool. List the cutting parameters you would use for mild steel workpieces.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An external (male) dovetail has a top width of **W = 50 mm**, a bottom width of **w = 30 mm**, and an included angle of **α = 60°**. Calculate the depth of the dovetail.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sketch Description (2 marks):**
The sketch should show a rectangular workpiece clamped in a swivel vice, with the form tool mounted on the tool head. The tool is ground to the exact dovetail profile. The vice is set horizontal (since the form tool itself contains the angle), and the cutting direction is along the length of the dovetail.

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. **Workpiece Preparation:** Mark out the dovetail boundary on the workpiece using a scriber and surface gauge. Punch reference marks.
2. **Vice Setup:** Mount the workpiece in a **swivel vice** with the marking aligned. For a 45° internal dovetail with a form tool, the vice remains horizontal (0°).
3. **Tool Selection and Mounting:** Grind a **form tool** to the exact dovetail profile (60° included for a 45° external mating). Mount on the shaper tool holder with correct rake angle.
4. **Stroke Adjustment:** Set the **stroke length** = dovetail length + 10 mm to allow clearance at both ends.
5. **Stroke Position:** Adjust the stroke position so that the tool clears the workpiece on the return stroke.
6. **Rough Cut:** Engage **automatic cross-feed at 0.3 mm per stroke**. Take multiple passes.
7. **Inspection:** After roughing, check the dovetail profile with a **dovetail gauge** or vernier.
8. **Finish Cut:** Reduce the feed to **0.05 mm per stroke** and take 1–2 finishing passes.
9. **Final Inspection:** Verify all dimensions, check for burrs, and deburr.

**Cutting Parameters for Mild Steel (1 mark):**
- Cutting speed: **v_c ≈ 15 m/min**
- Feed per stroke: **0.2 mm (roughing), 0.05 mm (finishing)**
- Depth of cut: **0.5 to 1.0 mm per pass**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** W = 50 mm, w = 30 mm, α = 60°.

**Find:** h.

**Step 1 — Master formula:**

$$h = \frac{W - w}{2\tan(\alpha/2)}$$

**Step 2 — Half-angle:**

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{60°}{2} = 30°$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$h = \frac{50 - 30}{2 \times \tan(30°)}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate tan(30°):**

$$\tan(30°) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} = 0.5774$$

**Step 5 — Compute:**

$$h = \frac{20}{2 \times 0.5774} = \frac{20}{1.1548} = 17.32 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{h \approx 17.32 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with a sketch the **difference between a male (external) and a female (internal) dovetail**. State one engineering application of each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A female dovetail is to be cut with a bottom width **w = 20 mm**, included angle **α = 45°**, and depth **h = 15 mm**. Calculate the top width **D** of the bore.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Sketch (3 marks):**

- **Male Dovetail:** A **protruding wedge** on a part that slides into a mating slot. The projection has a wide top and narrow root.
- **Female Dovetail:** A **recessed slot** machined into a part, with a narrow bottom and wide top opening.

**Tabular Comparison (3 marks):**

| Feature | Male Dovetail | Female Dovetail |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Protruding trapezoid | Recessed trapezoid |
| Top width vs Bottom | Top > Bottom | Top > Bottom (in absolute) |
| W (wide) | At the open tip | At the bore mouth |
| w (narrow) | At the root | At the slot bottom |
| Typical tool | Form tool (external) | Internal dovetail cutter or single point with swivel vice |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **Male Dovetail:** Dovetail slide of a milling machine cross-slide.
- **Female Dovetail:** Dovetail slot of a lathe cross-slide gib.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** w = 20 mm, α = 45°, h = 15 mm.

**Find:** D.

**Step 1 — Master formula for internal dovetail:**

$$D = w + 2h \tan\left(\frac{\alpha}{2}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Half-angle:**

$$\frac{\alpha}{2} = \frac{45°}{2} = 22.5°$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate tan(22.5°):**

$$\tan(22.5°) = 0.4142$$

**Step 4 — Compute the term 2h tan(α/2):**

$$2 \times 15 \times 0.4142 = 12.426 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 5 — Final addition:**

$$D = 20 + 12.426 = 32.426 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 32.43 \text{ mm}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up W and D:** The symbol **W** is used for the wide end of an **external** dovetail; **D** is used for the bore diameter (wide end) of an **internal** dovetail. Confusing these costs **2 marks** directly.
> 2. **Forgetting to halve the angle:** Students often write **tan(α)** instead of **tan(α/2)**. This is a classic 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the verification step:** For an internal dovetail, always confirm **D > w**. For an external dovetail, confirm **W > w**. The examiner awards marks for the verification comment.
> 4. **Not stating units:** Writing "17.32" instead of "17.32 mm" loses **0.5 mark** in the KTU scheme.
> 5. **Confusing clearance with rake angle:** Side **clearance angle (C)** is on the flank (relief). **Rake angle** is on the face (cutting). Do not interchange them.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A dovetail is a trapezoidal interlocking feature that resists axial pull-out through angled frictional contact.
- **Standard included angles:** **30°, 45°, 60°** — know them cold.
- **Master formula (Internal):** $D = w + 2h \tan(\alpha/2)$.
- **Master formula (External):** $W = w + 2h \tan(\alpha/2)$.
- **Inverse formula (depth):** $h = \dfrac{D - w}{2\tan(\alpha/2)}$.
- **Two methods of cutting:** (1) **Form tool** with vice horizontal, (2) **Single-point tool** with vice swiveled to **α/2**.
- **Side clearance angle:** Always provide **C = 3° to 8°** on the tool flank.
- **Rake angle of dovetail tool:** $\theta = 90° - \alpha/2 - C$.
- **Verification rule:** For internal dovetail, **D > w**; for external, **W > w**.
- **Mild steel cutting parameters:** Cutting speed **≈ 15 m/min**, feed **0.2 mm/stroke (rough)**, **0.05 mm/stroke (finish)**.
- **Inspection tool:** Use a **dovetail gauge** for angle and width verification.
- **Common mistake to avoid:** Never confuse the **half-angle** (α/2) with the **full included angle (α)** in trigonometry.
- **Self-locking principle:** Smaller **α** → stronger axial lock. α → 0° would theoretically lock infinitely, but is impractical to machine.
- **Real-world uses:** Slide gibs of lathes and milling machines, tool holders, modular jigs, and fixtures.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slotting of Keyway
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPEL407 - MACHINE TOOL LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Slotting of Keyway

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **Keyway** is a longitudinal or end slot machined along the axis of a shaft or inside a hub/bore, designed to accommodate a **key** — a small mechanical component that locks a shaft and a rotating element (gear, pulley, coupling) together to transmit torque without relative rotational motion.

**Slotting** is a machining operation performed on a **Slotter (also called a Vertical Shaper)** in which a single-point cutting tool mounted on a ram moves in a **linear reciprocating (vertical) motion** along the Z-axis, while the workpiece is held stationary on a circular/rectangular table. The cutting action occurs **only on the downward (forward) stroke**; the upward (return) stroke is idle and faster. Material is removed in the form of a chip through intermittent cutting.

The **Slotting of a Keyway** experiment typically involves machining an internal straight keyway on a cylindrical workpiece (shaft) mounted on a V-block or chuck, using a keyway slotting cutter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPEL407 - Module 2):** The student is expected to (i) identify the parts of a slotter, (ii) set up a workpiece and slotting tool, (iii) machine a keyway with given dimensions, and (iv) calculate machining time, cutting speed, and MRR using the **Quick Return Mechanism** formulation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are using a **manual chisel and hammer** to carve a vertical slot into a piece of wood clamped on a workbench.

- Your **hand pushing down** = the **ram of the slotter** carrying the cutting tool.
- The **downward push** = **cutting stroke** (slow, powerful, where the chip is removed).
- The **lifting up** = **return stroke** (fast, no cutting — saves time).
- The **small side-step** you give after each chisel blow = **feed per stroke**.
- The **V-block holding the wood round** = the **V-block / chuck** holding the shaft.

The slotter simply **automates this chiseling action** using a Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism, which makes the return stroke roughly **2 to 3 times faster** than the cutting stroke — this is why the slotter is so efficient for internal slots and keyways that cannot be machined by a horizontal milling cutter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not just use a horizontal shaper?** Because slotting is **vertical**, the **workpiece remains stationary and horizontal**. This is ideal for machining **internal keyways in gears, hubs, and large castings** where the workpiece is too heavy or awkwardly shaped to be reciprocated.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics (Industrial)

| Parameter | Typical Range |
|---|---|
| Quick Return Ratio (Q) | **1.5 to 2.5** |
| Max stroke length of slotter | **150 mm – 600 mm** |
| Slotting tool shank size (standard) | **16 mm, 20 mm, 25 mm, 32 mm, 40 mm** |
| Standard keyway widths (as per IS 2048) | **3, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18, 20 mm** |
| Spindle motor power | **1.5 kW – 5 kW** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle of a Slotter

A slotter converts the **uniform rotary motion of an electric motor** into **non-uniform reciprocating linear motion of the ram** using a **Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism** (also called crank-and-slotted-lever mechanism).

**Sequence of one operating cycle:**

1. **Forward (Cutting) Stroke** — Ram descends from top dead centre (TDC) to bottom dead centre (BDC). The cutting tool removes material. Velocity is **slow and nearly uniform** (mechanical advantage is high).
2. **Return Stroke** — Ram ascends from BDC back to TDC. **No cutting** occurs. Velocity is **2 to 2.5× faster**.
3. **Feed** — At the end of the return stroke (TDC), the workpiece (or tool cross-slide) is advanced laterally by a small amount called the **feed per stroke**.
4. **Repeat** for the required number of strokes until the desired keyway depth is achieved.

### 2.2 Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism — The 'Why' Behind It

- During forward stroke, the crank rotates through a **larger angle (θ₁ = α)**, where α > 90°.
- During return stroke, the crank rotates through a **smaller angle (θ₂ = 360° − α)**, where 360° − α < 90° (typically 60°–90°).
- Since the crank rotates at **uniform angular velocity**, the ram covers the same stroke length in different times → **return is faster**.

> **Mathematical relationship:**
>
> $$\text{Quick Return Ratio, } Q = \frac{T_f}{T_r} = \frac{\theta_1}{\theta_2} = \frac{\alpha}{360^\circ - \alpha}$$
>
> where $T_f$ = forward (cutting) stroke time, $T_r$ = return stroke time.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Spindle speed (n) | $n = \dfrac{1000 \times V_c}{\pi \times D}$ | rpm | For rotary tools (not directly used in slotter, but tool shank limits V_c) |
| 2 | Strokes per minute (N) | $N = \dfrac{1}{T_f + T_r}$ | strokes/min | From machine nameplate or table |
| 3 | Time per stroke (cycle) | $T_c = \dfrac{60}{N}$ | seconds | One full cycle |
| 4 | Forward stroke time | $T_f = \dfrac{Q}{1+Q} \times \dfrac{60}{N}$ | seconds | Where Q = quick return ratio |
| 5 | Return stroke time | $T_r = \dfrac{1}{1+Q} \times \dfrac{60}{N}$ | seconds | Faster of the two |
| 6 | Average cutting velocity | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{L \times N \times (1+Q)}{1000 \times Q}$ | m/min | L in mm |
| 7 | Feed per stroke | $f$ | mm/stroke | From feed dial; typically 0.05 – 0.5 mm |
| 8 | Total no. of strokes | $n_{tot} = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{f}$ | strokes | To achieve required keyway depth |
| 9 | Metal Removal Rate (MRR) | $MRR = L \times w \times f \times N$ | mm³/min | w = width of keyway = tool width |
| 10 | Specific Cutting Energy | $P_c = \dfrac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta}$ | kW | $U_s$ in N·m/mm²; η = efficiency (typically 0.7–0.8) |
| 11 | Total Machining Time | $T_m = \dfrac{n_{tot}}{N} = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{f \times N}$ | minutes | Keyway production time |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table notation:** All absolute / divide symbols inside cells are written as `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert V_c \vert$) to **preserve the markdown table syntax**. Do not write bare vertical bars.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Internal keyways** on gear hubs, couplings, and pulleys (post-boring operation).
- **T-slots** in machine tool tables.
- **Square and hexagonal holes** in die bodies.
- **Cam profiles** on flat surfaces.
- **Internal splines** in small batches.
- **Concave contours** that cannot be reached by a horizontal mill.

> A **keyway** is one of the most critical features in power transmission assemblies. Without it, no torque can be transmitted between a shaft and a gear in industrial machinery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Cutting Velocity with Quick Return Ratio

**Problem Statement:** A slotter makes 30 strokes/min with a stroke length of 100 mm. The quick return ratio is 2:1. Find the average cutting velocity during the forward stroke.

**Step 1 — Identify the given data**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 30 \text{ strokes/min} \\
L &= 100 \text{ mm} \\
Q &= \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} = \dfrac{2}{1} = 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the time of one complete cycle**

The crank completes **one revolution** in the time of one forward + one return stroke.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= \dfrac{60}{N} \\
&= \dfrac{60}{30} \\
&= 2 \text{ seconds per cycle}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Resolve into forward and return stroke times using the ratio Q**

Since $Q = T_f \div T_r$, we have $T_f = Q \cdot T_r$. Also $T_f + T_r = T_c$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q \cdot T_r + T_r &= T_c \\
T_r (Q + 1) &= T_c \\
T_r &= \dfrac{T_c}{Q + 1} \\
T_r &= \dfrac{2}{2 + 1} \\
&= \dfrac{2}{3} = 0.667 \text{ seconds (return)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_f &= Q \cdot T_r \\
&= 2 \times \dfrac{2}{3} \\
&= \dfrac{4}{3} = 1.333 \text{ seconds (forward / cutting)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute average cutting velocity during forward stroke**

Average velocity = stroke length ÷ forward stroke time. Convert mm to m by dividing by 1000, and seconds to minutes by multiplying numerator's time factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \dfrac{L}{T_f} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ m}}{1000 \text{ mm}} \times \dfrac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{1.333} \times \dfrac{60}{1000} \\
&= 75.02 \times 0.06 \\
&= 4.501 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Verification using the compact formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \dfrac{L \times N \times (1 + Q)}{1000 \times Q} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \times 30 \times (1 + 2)}{1000 \times 2} \\
&= \dfrac{100 \times 30 \times 3}{2000} \\
&= \dfrac{9000}{2000} \\
&= 4.5 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ **Result matches Step 4. Average cutting velocity = 4.5 m/min.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Examples

#### **Example A — Total Machining Time**

A keyway of **length 60 mm, width 10 mm, depth 5 mm** is to be cut on a slotter running at **25 strokes/min**, with a feed of **0.2 mm/stroke**. Find the total machining time and MRR.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{No. of strokes required} &= \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{feed}} = \dfrac{5}{0.2} = 25 \text{ strokes} \\
T_m &= \dfrac{25}{25} = 1 \text{ minute} \\
MRR &= L \times w \times f \times N \\
&= 60 \times 10 \times 0.2 \times 25 \\
&= 3000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} \\
&= 3 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Example B — Power Estimation**

Using the same setup, with **Specific Cutting Energy $U_s = 2.5 \text{ N·m/mm}^2$** and **mechanical efficiency $\eta = 0.75$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_c &= \dfrac{MRR \times U_s}{60 \times 1000 \times \eta} \\
&= \dfrac{3000 \times 2.5}{60000 \times 0.75} \\
&= \dfrac{7500}{45000} \\
&= 0.1667 \text{ kW} \\
&\approx 167 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Example C — Strokes Required for a Longer Keyway**

For a **100 mm long, 6 mm deep** keyway at **feed = 0.15 mm/stroke**, slotter running at **40 strokes/min**, $Q = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{tot} &= \dfrac{6}{0.15} = 40 \text{ strokes} \\
T_m &= \dfrac{40}{40} = 1 \text{ minute} \\
V_{avg} &= \dfrac{100 \times 40 \times (1+2)}{1000 \times 2} = \dfrac{12000}{2000} = 6 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Error & Sensitivity Check

| Variation | Effect on $T_m$ | Effect on MRR | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Feed (f) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | More material per stroke, but poorer surface finish |
| ↑ Stroke rate (N) | ↓ Decreases | ↑ Increases | Higher productivity, more tool wear, higher temperature |
| ↑ Quick return ratio (Q) | ↓ $T_f$ reduces, but $T_c$ unchanged | ↑ Slightly higher $V_{avg}$ | More time spent cutting per cycle |
| ↑ Depth of keyway | ↑ Increases | Constant | More strokes needed |
| ↑ Tool width (w) | Constant | ↑ Increases | Wider tool removes more material per stroke |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a **slotter** and list **any four operations** that can be performed on it.

**Model Answer:**
A slotter is a reciprocating-type machine tool similar to a shaper but with a vertical ram. The cutting tool moves up and down while the workpiece is held stationary on a circular table. It is primarily used for machining internal surfaces, slots, and keyways.

**Four operations:**
1. Internal keyway cutting
2. Square / hexagonal hole making
3. Internal grooves and T-slots
4. Cam and contour profile machining

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is a **quick return ratio (Q)**? Write its significance in slotter operation.

**Model Answer:**
Quick return ratio is the ratio of time taken for the cutting (forward) stroke to the time taken for the return stroke.

$$Q = \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} = \dfrac{\theta_f}{\theta_r}$$

Typical value: **Q = 1.5 to 2.5**.

**Significance:** Since cutting happens only on the forward stroke, a higher Q means more time is spent on actual cutting per minute, improving productivity. It also means the return stroke (idle) is completed faster, saving cycle time.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a Whitworth quick return mechanism** used in a slotter. Derive the relationship between quick return ratio and crank angles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism:**

It consists of a **crank disc** rotating about a fixed centre, a **slotted lever** oscillating about a fulcrum, and a **connecting rod** transmitting motion to the ram.

**Working:**
- The crank rotates at uniform angular velocity driven by the bull gear.
- A **slider (pin)** on the crank disc slides within the slot of the lever.
- As the crank rotates, the slider pushes the lever, which oscillates about its fulcrum.
- The end of the lever is connected to the **ram** through the connecting rod, producing vertical reciprocation.

**Derivation:**

Let:
- $\alpha$ = angle turned by crank during forward (cutting) stroke
- $\beta$ = angle turned by crank during return stroke = $360° - \alpha$

Since crank rotates at uniform angular velocity $\omega$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Time of forward stroke, } T_f &= \dfrac{\alpha}{\omega} \\
\text{Time of return stroke, } T_r &= \dfrac{\beta}{\omega} = \dfrac{360° - \alpha}{\omega}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q = \dfrac{T_f}{T_r} = \dfrac{\alpha}{\beta} = \dfrac{\alpha}{360° - \alpha}
$$

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q (360° - \alpha) &= \alpha \\
360 Q - Q \alpha &= \alpha \\
360 Q &= \alpha (1 + Q) \\
\alpha &= \dfrac{360 Q}{1 + Q} \text{ degrees}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $Q = 2$: $\alpha = \dfrac{720}{3} = 240°$, and $\beta = 360° - 240° = 120°$. Hence, the crank spends **240° on cutting** and **120° on return** in each revolution.

**(b)** A slotter has a **stroke length of 120 mm**, makes **25 strokes/min**, and the quick return ratio is **3:1**. Calculate **(i)** the average cutting velocity during the forward stroke and **(ii)** the total machining time required to cut a keyway **8 mm deep** at a feed of **0.2 mm/stroke**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L = 120$ mm
- $N = 25$ strokes/min
- $Q = 3$
- Depth $= 8$ mm, Feed $f = 0.2$ mm/stroke

**Part (i) — Average cutting velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \dfrac{L \times N \times (1 + Q)}{1000 \times Q} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \times 25 \times (1 + 3)}{1000 \times 3} \\
&= \dfrac{120 \times 25 \times 4}{3000} \\
&= \dfrac{12000}{3000} \\
&= 4 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Total machining time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{tot} &= \dfrac{\text{depth}}{\text{feed}} = \dfrac{8}{0.2} = 40 \text{ strokes} \\
T_m &= \dfrac{n_{tot}}{N} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.6 \text{ minutes} \\
&= 1 \text{ min } 36 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**MRR (bonus calculation):**

$$
MRR = L \times w \times f \times N = 120 \times 10 \times 0.2 \times 25 = 6000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 6 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step procedure to set up a workpiece and slotting tool for cutting an internal keyway on a shaft** mounted on a V-block. Mention any **four safety precautions**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure:**

1. **Marking:** Using a height gauge and scriber, mark the centre line of the keyway along the length of the shaft.
2. **Workpiece Holding:** Place the shaft on a V-block of suitable size on the circular table. Clamp using step clamps and T-bolts.
3. **Stroke Setting:** Adjust the **crank disc radius** to obtain a stroke length **5–10 mm longer** than the keyway length. This ensures the tool clears the workpiece on the return stroke.
4. **Stroke Position:** Adjust the **ram position** so that the tool tip starts cutting just above the workpiece surface (top dead centre).
5. **Tool Selection:** Choose a slotting tool whose width matches the keyway width (e.g., 10 mm keyway → 10 mm tool).
6. **Tool Mounting:** Mount the tool in the tool post with minimum overhang. Set the tool to **clear the workpiece** during return stroke.
7. **Alignment:** Using a **dial indicator**, align the tool tip with the scribed line on the shaft.
8. **Test Run:** Start the machine at slow speed. Engage half nut. Apply cutting fluid.
9. **Cutting & Feeding:** After each return stroke, give a feed of **0.1–0.3 mm** using the cross-slide handwheel.
10. **Depth Check:** Periodically stop the machine (with tool at BDC) and measure the depth using a **depth micrometer**.
11. **Finishing:** Once depth is reached, give 2–3 light passes for better surface finish.
12. **Inspection:** Use a **keyway gauge / internal vernier** to verify width and parallelism.

**Safety Precautions (any four):**

1. **Wear safety goggles and apron** before starting the machine.
2. **Ensure all clamps and bolts are tight** before starting.
3. **Never adjust stroke or feed while the machine is running.**
4. **Keep hands away from the cutting zone** — use a brush to clear chips.
5. **Use coolant cautiously** and ensure proper guard is in place.
6. **Stop the machine** before taking any measurements.

---

**(b)** A keyway of **length 75 mm, width 8 mm, depth 4 mm** is to be cut on a slotter. The slotter runs at **30 strokes/min** with $Q = 2$. Feed = 0.25 mm/stroke. Calculate: **(i)** MRR, **(ii)** Total strokes required, **(iii)** Total machining time, **(iv)** Average cutting velocity. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $L = 75$ mm, $w = 8$ mm, depth = 4 mm, $N = 30$ strokes/min, $Q = 2$, $f = 0.25$ mm/stroke.

**(i) MRR:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
MRR &= L \times w \times f \times N \\
&= 75 \times 8 \times 0.25 \times 30 \\
&= 4500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min} = 4.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total strokes:**

$$
n_{tot} = \dfrac{\text{depth}}{f} = \dfrac{4}{0.25} = 16 \text{ strokes}
$$

**(iii) Machining time:**

$$
T_m = \dfrac{16}{30} = 0.533 \text{ min} \approx 32 \text{ seconds}
$$

**(iv) Average cutting velocity:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{avg} &= \dfrac{L \times N \times (1 + Q)}{1000 \times Q} \\
&= \dfrac{75 \times 30 \times 3}{1000 \times 2} \\
&= \dfrac{6750}{2000} \\
&= 3.375 \text{ m/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the (1 + Q) factor** in the $V_{avg}$ formula is the most common mistake — students write $V = LN/1000$ which is the **uniform velocity**, NOT the slotter's actual cutting velocity.
> 2. **Mixing up units:** Length must be in **mm** (since dividing by 1000) and time must be in **minutes** when using the compact $V_{avg}$ formula.
> 3. **Stroke vs. Spindle RPM:** Slotter has **strokes/min**, not RPM. Do NOT use the $V = \pi D N$ formula.
> 4. **Feed direction confusion:** In slotting, feed is given **at the top of the return stroke (TDC)**, not continuously. The cutting happens only on the downward stroke.
> 5. **MRR units confusion:** Always convert mm³/min to cm³/min (÷1000) for clarity in records.
> 6. **Forgetting tool overhang:** A long tool overhang causes **chatter and breakage** — KTU examiners deduct marks if this isn't mentioned in the procedure.

---

### ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔑 **Slotter = Vertical Shaper** with stationary workpiece and reciprocating tool.
- 🔑 **Cutting action only on forward (downward) stroke**; return stroke is idle and faster.
- 🔑 **Whitworth Quick Return Mechanism** provides $Q = 1.5$ to $2.5$.
- 🔑 **Master formula:** $V_{avg} = \dfrac{L \times N \times (1 + Q)}{1000 \times Q}$ m/min (L in mm).
- 🔑 **MRR** = $L \times w \times f \times N$ mm³/min.
- 🔑 **Machining time** = depth ÷ (feed × N) minutes.
- 🔑 **Keyway tool width = keyway width** (no radial compensation needed).
- 🔑 **Stroke length must exceed keyway length** by 5–10 mm to clear the workpiece.
- 🔑 **Feed per stroke**: 0.05 to 0.5 mm; feed at **TDC** of return stroke.
- 🔑 **Alignment is done with dial indicator** on the scribed keyway line.
- 🔑 **Use V-block + clamps** for cylindrical shafts.
- 🔑 **Always use cutting fluid** for slotting mild steel to improve tool life and finish.
- 🔑 **Safety first:** Goggles, no loose clothing, no adjustment during running.
- 🔑 **Lab record must include:** neat sketch, tool setup, calculations, and tabulated results.
